N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDSTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)---CHAPTER 1: THE BELLThe bell rang wrong.Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.He didn’t go back for it.His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.One. Two. Three. Four.The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.Copper.Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.He didn't.Underface rule: waste nothing.His hands started shaking.Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.And then the tremor came back twice as fast.It wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.The tremor kept going.He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.Still shaking.It wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."Jhace looked at the structure.The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.The people inside didn't have minutes."I'm not—" Jhace started.The heat arrived.Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.His chest burned hotter.The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.He didn't know what he was doing.He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.He put both hands on the main support column.Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.The building stopped moving.It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count."Move," he rasped. "Get them out."Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.She didn't look at him like a miracle.She looked at him like a system that could fail."How long can you hold?" she demanded.Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know.""Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away."I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth."Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.One hundred.One hundred twenty.His arms started to tremble.Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else."Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.He didn't answer. Answering took breath.At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash."How many?" he rasped.The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.Jhace's hands shook harder."No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb."Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission."Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.Jhace let the filament go.The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.He staggered back into open air.His hands wouldn't stop shaking.The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name.""Jhace Torrins."She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two-forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway."Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain."If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.It didn’t. Not fully.A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.He nodded once."Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."The woman's slate scratched.She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.Behind them, the bell rang again.Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.---CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERNThe theodolite wouldn't settle.Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.The crosshair still drifted.No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.Vibration.Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:Tide 0 — Kindling  Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.Her mouth tasted copper.She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went wi[... ELLIPSIZATION ...]: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.Chloen read the first line.YOU CAN SAY NO.She looked up. “That’s new.”“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.The door clicked. The room held its breath.Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”He left.Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.Tiffani didn’t move.She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.---CODA: GLASS IN THE HANDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.Jhace tried to sleep.Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.---CODA: THE SHARDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.Then the breathing stopped.No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.The door opened anyway.Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.A figure stepped in.No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.They didn't speak.They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.Jhace tried to jerk away.Nothing.He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.Nothing.The figure took that hand anyway.Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.Something cold settled into the center of his palm.A shard.Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.Light threaded up from his palm.Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.But he felt the line.He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.Then they stepped back.No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.The door shut without sound.The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.The tremor was gone.In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.---END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS---

Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.He didn’t go back for it.His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.One. Two. Three. Four.The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.Copper.Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.He didn't.Underface rule: waste nothing.His hands started shaking.Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.And then the tremor came back twice as fast.It wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.The tremor kept going.He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.Still shaking.It wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."Jhace looked at the structure.The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.The people inside didn't have minutes."I'm not—" Jhace started.The heat arrived.Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.His chest burned hotter.The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.He didn't know what he was doing.He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.He put both hands on the main support column.Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.The building stopped moving.It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count."Move," he rasped. "Get them out."Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.She didn't look at him like a miracle.She looked at him like a system that could fail."How long can you hold?" she demanded.Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know.""Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away."I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth."Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.One hundred.One hundred twenty.His arms started to tremble.Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else."Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.He didn't answer. Answering took breath.At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash."How many?" he rasped.The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.Jhace's hands shook harder."No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb."Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission."Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.Jhace let the filament go.The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.He staggered back into open air.His hands wouldn't stop shaking.The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name.""Jhace Torrins."She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two-forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway."Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain."If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.It didn’t. Not fully.A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.He nodded once."Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."The woman's slate scratched.She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.Behind them, the bell rang again.Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.---CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERNThe theodolite wouldn't settle.Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.The crosshair still drifted.No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.Vibration.Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:Tide 0 — Kindling  Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.Her mouth tasted copper.She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This[... ELLIPSIZATION ...] DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.Chloen read the first line.YOU CAN SAY NO.She looked up. “That’s new.”“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.The door clicked. The room held its breath.Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”He left.Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.Tiffani didn’t move.She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.---CODA: GLASS IN THE HANDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.Jhace tried to sleep.Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.---CODA: THE SHARDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.Then the breathing stopped.No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.The door opened anyway.Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.A figure stepped in.No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.They didn't speak.They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.Jhace tried to jerk away.Nothing.He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.Nothing.The figure took that hand anyway.Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.Something cold settled into the center of his palm.A shard.Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.Light threaded up from his palm.Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.But he felt the line.He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.Then they stepped back.No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.The door shut without sound.The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.The tremor was gone.In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.---END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS---

[TOOL]
CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid-hum without being crushed by it.
Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.
Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.
Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.
His hands shook.
The grid-hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.
He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.
He stared at it like it had betrayed him.
Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”
“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.
“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”
Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.
He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.
“What did you call it out there?” he asked.
Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.
Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste  
Light manifestation: filament contact-dependent  
Tremor: post-termination, permanent marker
“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”
Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”
“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”
Jhace’s jaw worked.
He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.
He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”
Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.
She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational.
“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.

A knock hit the workshop door.
Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.
“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”
Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”
“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”
Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”
“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”
Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”
Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”
“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.
Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”
Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”
“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”
Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”
Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”
Jhace breathed in, tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.
“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
---

[ASSISTANT]
Chapter 4 is **reopened**.

## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL

Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.

Tiffani sat on an overturned crate. Slate on her knees. Left leg stretched out like a brace. The knee had started locking after long stands on stone floors.

She didn’t lean back.

Always ready to move. Always ready to write.

Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.

His hands shook.

The grid didn’t fail with a bang. It stuttered. Missed beats. Caught up. Like an old machine slipping teeth.

He tried again.

The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.

He stared at it like it had betrayed him.

Tiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”

“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick it up. His fingertips felt numb where the filament had burned them. Not pain.

Absence.

“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani said. “It thinks you’re still holding.”

Jhace set the bolt down. Flexed his hand hard.

The tremor didn’t care.

He hated that. Hated the way his own muscles had become unreliable.

“What did you call it out there?” he asked.

Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. A crude timeline. No poetry. Just sequence.

**Activation onset:** chest heat + copper taste  
**Light manifestation:** filament, contact-dependent  
**Tremor:** post-termination, permanent marker

“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Right now it’s just stories.”

“And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”

“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the word **permanent**. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”

Jhace’s jaw worked.

He had bargained with Junction Seven. Told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.

He looked at his shaking hands.

“Stage One,” he said. Low. “Tremor.”

Tiffani nodded and wrote:

**Stage One:** tremor + filament residue. **Functional. Marked.**

She stared at **Functional** like it offended her.

Drew a hard line through it.

Rewrote: **Operational.**

“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.

A knock hit the workshop door.

Bram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded like a bad habit.

“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”

Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”

“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”

Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”

“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay.” Bram stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”

Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”

“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.

Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”

“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”

Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”

Bram pointed at her slate. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”

Jhace breathed in.

Tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes.

Felt the city’s hum through the floorboards like a second pulse.

“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”

---

[USER]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL — LOCKED

---

Jhace's workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.

Tiffani sat on an overturned crate. Slate on her knees. Left leg extended to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors.

She didn't lean back.

Always ready to move. Always ready to write.

Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.

His hands shook.

The grid didn't fail with a bang. It stuttered. Missed beats. Caught up. Like an old machine slipping teeth.

He tried again.

The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.

He stared at it like it had betrayed him.

Tiffani didn't look up. "Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion."

"Because I'm exerting," Jhace muttered, bending to pick it up. His fingertips felt numb where the filament had burned them. Not pain.

Absence.

"Because your body is remembering," Tiffani said. "It thinks you're still holding."

Jhace set the bolt down. Flexed his hand hard.

The tremor didn't care.

He hated that. Hated the way his own muscles had become unreliable.

"What did you call it out there?" he asked.

Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. A crude timeline. No poetry. Just sequence.

Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste
Light manifestation: filament, contact-dependent
Tremor: post-termination, permanent marker

"I need a taxonomy," she said. "Stages. Right now it's just stories."

"And you think writing stages keeps people alive."

"I think it keeps them from lying to themselves." She tapped the word permanent. "You can't call it temporary. You can't bargain with it."

Jhace's jaw worked.

He had bargained with Junction Seven. Told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn't need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.

He looked at his shaking hands.

"Stage One," he said. Low. "Tremor."

Tiffani nodded and wrote:

Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.

She stared at Functional like it offended her.

Drew a hard line through it.

Rewrote: Operational.

"Functional makes it sound polite," she muttered.

A knock hit the workshop door.

Bram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded like a bad habit.

"Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat," Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. "City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb."

Jhace didn't look up. "Who's asking?"

"Crown's asking questions," Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. "Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt."

Tiffani's pen paused. "What questions."

"Names. Locations. 'Verified capability.' That sort of foreplay." Bram stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. "Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he's washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone."

Jhace's jaw tightened. "To do what."

Bram spread his hands. "To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you're popular."

"We don't belong to any of them," Tiffani said.

Bram laughed, quick and wrong. "Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining 'we don't belong' while a ceiling is coming down and someone's screaming for a hold. They'll file you under 'non-compliant' and act like that's a weather condition."

Jhace's tremor ticked in his fingers. "So you're here to warn us."

"I'm here to tell you the door is already being measured," Bram said. "I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are 'public infrastructure.'"

Tiffani's mouth went flat. "Then we build methodology fast."

Bram pointed at her slate. "Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it's going to read like a job description. And you won't like the benefits package."

Jhace breathed in.

Tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes.

Felt the city's hum through the floorboards.

"Then we build it fast," he said. "Before they decide to build it for us."

**END CHAPTER 4**

## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK

The kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.

The bell came late.

That was the first problem.

When Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had over-pressured the feed line to meet quota and called it “necessary.”

A foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”

Jhace moved by habit.

Chest heat answered like a dog hearing its name.

Tiffani caught his sleeve.

“Not yet.”

Jhace turned his head a fraction. The moment he shifted focus, the warmth behind his sternum flared—punishment for looking away from the load. The tremor in his hands ticked harder, like it wanted permission.

“People are inside,” he rasped.

“People are leaving,” she said, pointing.

She was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.

“We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened.

He hated needing permission to do the only thing that had worked.

He dropped to one knee anyway.

Wedge slips. Strap loops. Collar brackets. The old tools. The honest ones.

His first wedge went in crooked.

He felt it before he saw it. The angle wrong. The bite uneven.

He yanked it back out. Knuckles scraped stone. Grainy dust cut under his nail.

The foreman saw.

The workers saw.

That was the second problem.

He wasn’t a clean solution anymore. He was a man with shaking hands making visible mistakes.

He shoved the wedge in again. Straight this time. Hammered it home with the heel of his palm because the mallet handle wouldn’t stay still.

Bram’s crew swarmed the brace line behind him, hands fast, straps ready, mouths full of curses and grit.

For once, labor did what labor could do.

The gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.

A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he couldn’t decide whether touching the structure made him brave or stupid.

Corven.

Jhace had seen him in the tunnels. Quiet. Competent. Always watching other men’s hands.

Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”

“Louder,” Jhace finished.

Corven nodded hard. “In my teeth.”

Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”

Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”

“Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”

The wedges held for two breath-counts.

Then the central column shifted.

Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The brace line bowed like it was thinking about surrender.

Workers were still inside.

“Tiff,” Jhace said, voice rough.

She didn’t hesitate.

“Now.”

Jhace put both hands on the column.

Heat flared behind his sternum—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and copper-gold filament threaded into the seam like the stone had been waiting for him.

The column stopped shifting.

The groan of stressed iron went quiet.

Jhace’s breath shook out of him. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, trying to walk itself sideways. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.

“Keep moving!” Bram shouted, voice cutting through the noise like a saw. “If you stop, you become debris! Count them out! Loud. Fast!”

Bodies poured out. Coughing. Limping. Dragging each other by collars.

Tiffani didn’t count in her head. She counted with her mouth. Audible. Aggressive. Like numbers could bully stone into patience.

“One. Two. Three—”

Corven stared at Jhace’s hands.

Jhace kept his eyes forward. “Your turn.”

Corven flinched.

“Secondary brace,” Jhace said, through clenched teeth. “Touch. Don’t squeeze. Don’t try to be a hero.”

Corven stepped in and laid both palms against the brace.

His breath hitched.

Jhace saw it in his face before he heard it: copper taste arriving like a slap.

Corven swallowed hard. “Copper.”

Tiffani’s pen scratched.

Corven’s eyes widened. “Chest—”

Heat took him.

Not metaphor. Not fear. Actual furnace bloom behind the ribs.

Corven made a sound like he’d been punched.

Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded from Corven’s hands into the brace line.

The brace stopped moving.

Corven’s hands started to shake immediately.

Not after. Not later.

Now.

“Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”

Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”

“Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”

The tremor in Jhace’s arms surged in sympathy. His shoulders burned. Vision tunneled at the edges, copper-gold sparks skating across the periphery.

“How long?” Tiffani asked.

Jhace didn’t answer in words. He answered in counting. Heartbeats ticking behind his teeth.

Eighty.

One-twenty.

One-fifty.

The last workers stumbled out—two hundred-plus bodies alive because they’d been allowed time.

“Clear!” someone shouted.

Tiffani’s voice cut clean. “Terminate.”

Jhace released first.

The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down and the pressure line had been throttled. Not safe. Not fixed. But no longer falling while people were inside.

Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.

Jhace caught him by the elbow. The tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable, like even saving someone standing in front of him required negotiation with his nerves.

Corven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. “It’s not stopping.”

“It doesn’t,” Jhace said.

Corven stared like he expected the sentence to end with a condition.

It didn’t.

Tiffani wrote the numbers anyway.

**Evacuated: 219.**  
**Casualties: 0.**  
**Operators marked: 2.**

Bram approached, face hard under the dust. “Crown’s going to hear about this. And they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”

Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”

Corven swallowed again, still tasting copper like a curse. “How long does it last?”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”

Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. Something worse. “So never.”

“Yeah.”

Corven set his palm on a railing. The tremor rattled bone against metal.

He pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.

It kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.

Tiffani’s pen didn’t pause. Because pausing didn’t change what was true.

---

### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)

The count was an argument before it was a number.

The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.

Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.

Jhace was already marked from the gantry. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.

Tiffani stared at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.

Two operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, failure points.

One operator at two-forty meant simplicity.

A clean line.

“Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell.”

Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty.

Functional.

They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, like a warning that didn’t care who listened.

Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker posture in a glassworker frame.

Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not comfort. Numbers didn’t soften just because people begged.

One hundred.

One-forty.

One-eighty.

At one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.

The evacuation lane kinked.

The next family stumbled.

The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket like a mouth deciding to bite down.

Three people were on the wrong side when it did.

Two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.

Tiffani saw them and for a heartbeat the slate in her hands felt obscene.

“Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”

Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.

Two-ten.

Two-twenty.

Two-thirty.

The pocket didn’t clear.

The child’s coughing turned wet.

At two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway.

Because protocols existed for a reason.

Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.

Senne released.

The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.

They dug for hours.

They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.

Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.

**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  
**Evacuated:** 47  
**Casualties:** 3  
**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.

She wrote *cause* as if precision could make it reversible.

Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.

Nothing came out.

She stared at the number.

Three.

Not a count.

Three names she would never know.

The chalk snapped in her grip.

She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:

**Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**

Underlined it twice.

Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn’t behave.

Her fingers kept shaking anyway.

---

[USER]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

---

The kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.

That was the first problem.

Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, trying to walk itself sideways. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.

"Keep moving!" Bram shouted, voice cutting through the noise like a saw. "If you stop, you become debris! Count them out! Loud. Fast!"

Bodies poured out. Coughing. Limping. Dragging each other by collars.

Tiffani didn't count in her head. She counted with her mouth. Audible. Aggressive. Like numbers could bully stone into patience.

"One. Two. Three—"

Corven stared at Jhace's hands.

Jhace kept his eyes forward. "Your turn."

Corven flinched.

"Secondary brace," Jhace said, through clenched teeth. "Touch. Don't squeeze. Don't try to be a hero."

Corven stepped in and laid both palms against the brace.

His breath hitched.

Jhace saw it in his face before he heard it: copper taste arriving like a slap.

Corven swallowed hard. "Copper."

Tiffani's pen scratched.

Corven's eyes widened. "Chest—"

Heat took him.

Not metaphor. Not fear. Actual furnace bloom behind the ribs.

Corven made a sound like he'd been punched.

Then light—thinner than Jhace's, but real—threaded from Corven's hands into the brace line.

The brace stopped moving.

Corven's hands started to shake immediately.

Not after. Not later.

Now.

"Stage One onset during hold," Tiffani murmured, writing. "Early marker."

Corven's jaw clenched. "I can do—"

"Do exactly what the structure needs," Jhace snapped. "Nothing more."

The tremor in Jhace's arms surged in sympathy. His shoulders burned. Vision tunneled at the edges, copper-gold sparks skating across the periphery.

"How long?" Tiffani asked.

Jhace didn't answer in words. He answered in counting. Heartbeats ticking behind his teeth.

Eighty.

One-twenty.

One-fifty.

The last workers stumbled out—two hundred-plus bodies alive because they'd been allowed time.

"Clear!" someone shouted.

Tiffani's voice cut clean. "Terminate."

Jhace released first.

The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down and the pressure line had been throttled. Not safe. Not fixed. But no longer falling while people were inside.

Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.

Jhace caught him by the elbow. The tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable, like even saving someone standing in front of him required negotiation with his nerves.

Corven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. "It's not stopping."

"It doesn't," Jhace said.

Corven stared like he expected the sentence to end with a condition.

It didn't.

Tiffani wrote the numbers anyway.

**Evacuated: 219.**
**Casualties: 0.**
**Operators marked: 2.**

Bram approached, face hard under the dust. "Crown's going to hear about this. And they'll bring forms instead of shovels."

Tiffani didn't look up. "Good."

Corven swallowed again. Still tasting copper.

"How long does it last?" he asked.

Jhace's jaw tightened. "Until you stop manifesting."

Corven's mouth twitched. Not a smile. Something worse. "So never."

"Yeah."

Corven set his palm on a railing. The tremor rattled bone against metal.

He pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.

It kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.

Tiffani's pen didn't pause. Because pausing didn't change what was true.

---

### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)

The count was an argument before it was a number.

The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.

Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.

Jhace was already marked from the gantry. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.

Tiffani stared at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.

Two operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, failure points.

One operator at two-forty meant simplicity.

A clean line.

"Single deployment," she said. "Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell."

Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty.

Functional.

They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, a warning that didn't care who listened.

Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker posture in a glassworker frame.

Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not comfort. Numbers didn't soften just because people begged.

One hundred.

One-forty.

One-eighty.

At one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn't walked because she'd trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.

The evacuation lane kinked.

The next family stumbled.

The stone didn't collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.

Three people were on the wrong side when it did.

Two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.

Tiffani saw them and for a heartbeat the slate in her hands felt obscene.

"Hold," she said, voice flat. "Keep holding."

Senne's eyes didn't move. She couldn't spare the motion.

Two-ten.

Two-twenty.

Two-thirty.

The pocket didn't clear.

The child's coughing turned wet.

At two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway.

Because protocols existed for a reason.

Because if she didn't, she would never stop.

Senne released.

The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.

They dug for hours.

They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.

Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.

**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats
**Evacuated:** 47
**Casualties:** 3
**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.

She wrote *cause* as if precision could make it reversible.

Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.

Nothing came out.

She stared at the number.

Three.

Not a count.

Three names she would never know.

The chalk snapped in her grip.

She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:

**Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**

Underlined it twice.

Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave.

Her fingers kept shaking anyway.

---

**END CHAPTER 5**

---
