[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS — VARIANT A

---

Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.

That was the first thing Jhace noticed.

Not the lack of robe—plenty of men worked without ceremony. It was the badge. Bright. Clean. Pinned high where it couldn’t be missed. A symbol meant to make doors open and mouths close.

The second thing was how Corran didn’t look tired.

No grit in his hairline. No dust ground into the seams of his coat. No red rim around the eyes from too many bells and not enough breath.

Like he’d been watching collapse from behind glass.

Jhace’s workshop smelled like quench water and iron filings and blood that had dried wrong.

Tiffani sat at the bench with her slate open to the route failure.

Three.

She’d underlined the spotter rule twice. The chalk grooves were deep enough to catch a fingernail.

Jhace stood with his palms flat on the wood, trying to pin his hands to something that wouldn’t shake.

It didn’t work.

Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s tremor.

“For the record,” Corran said, before anyone asked anything, “I’m not here to stop you.”

Bram Kerris hovered by the door, half inside, half ready to vanish. He’d stopped smiling two bells ago.

Tiffani didn’t stand. She didn’t offer a crate. She didn’t perform courtesy for badges.

“Then why are you here?” she asked.

“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”

Bram let out a dry little breath. “There it is.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Crown.”

Corran didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”

Bram lifted his hands in a slow, mocking blessing. “The holy trinity. Prayer. Production. Paperwork.”

Tiffani shot him a look without looking away from Corran. “Names.”

Corran stepped farther into the workshop like he had the right to breathe the heat. He didn’t touch anything. Didn’t smear dust into their work. Clean hands stayed clean.

“Administrator Corlexi,” he said. “Infrastructure Compliance.”

Jhace felt the tremor tick higher at the name. Not fear.

Anger.

“He was already here,” Jhace said. “He already offered.”

Corran nodded once. “Voluntary. For now.”

Tiffani’s pen moved. She didn’t ask permission. The scratch of it was the only sound that behaved.

Corran’s eyes flicked to her slate. To the line that said **Casualties: 3**. To the break in chalk that still lay on the bench like a snapped tooth.

“Three,” he said.

Tiffani didn’t correct him. Didn’t justify it.

“Secondary beam shear,” she said. Flat. “Map was wrong.”

Corran’s mouth tightened. “Maps are always wrong. People forget that.”

Jhace tasted copper at the back of his tongue.

Not flood. Not activation.

Just the low, stubborn tang that had been living there since the sky split.

Corran reached into his coat and pulled out a folded packet.

Paper. Old weight. Archive stock.

Not a slate page. Not a workshop note. Something that had been handled by offices and sealed by hands that never touched rubble.

He set it on the bench between them.

Tiffani didn’t touch it immediately.

“What is that?” she asked.

“Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”

Jhace’s chest heat stirred behind his sternum like a coal shifting in ash.

Tiffani’s eyes sharpened. “How many?”

“Enough.” Corran’s voice stayed level. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”

Bram muttered, “That’s comforting.”

Tiffani opened the packet.

Her eyes moved fast. Too fast. She read like she was trying to outrun what she already suspected.

Jhace watched her face change in small, controlled fractures: jaw tightening. A blink held one heartbeat too long. A breath she didn’t fully release.

Numbers did that to her. Not because she loved them.

Because they didn’t let her lie.

“This one,” she said, tapping a page. “Survival past lock. How?”

Corran didn’t look proud of having an answer.

“He didn’t terminate,” he said. “He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway.”

Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the heat of the forge.

Locked hold.

The phrase sat in his skull like a tool you couldn’t put down.

“What happens to the body?” Jhace asked.

Corran didn’t hesitate. “Seizure. Blackout. Respiratory failure. Heart irregularity. Sometimes the light keeps going while the person is already dead.”

Bram swallowed. The sound was loud in the quiet.

Tiffani’s pen scratched:

**Stage Three marker:** termination failure possible.  
**Locked hold:** manifestation continues under autonomic collapse.  
**Outcome:** survival rare. Post-hold function catastrophic.

Jhace stared at his hands.

They shook, yes. But they still listened when he told them to clench.

He imagined a Tide where they didn’t.

A Tide where his body became a platform for the hold to continue without him.

His mouth went dry and copper at the same time.

Tiffani looked up from the packet. “These cases—were they all contact holds?”

Corran nodded. “Hands. Forearms. Sometimes full-body brace when they couldn’t find a seam.”

“And the hunger?” Tiffani asked, like saying the word didn’t make it true, but not saying it didn’t make it go away.

Corran’s gaze flicked to Jhace’s sternum. Like he could see the coal.

“Yes,” he said. “They describe it differently. But it’s the same thing. The body starts wanting the hold.”

Jhace felt the warmth behind his ribs tighten.

A warm twist. A gentle pull.

Not pain.

Invitation.

His tremor eased for a single heartbeat.

Jhace froze.

He hadn’t moved. Hadn’t braced. Hadn’t held anything.

The tremor had just… softened.

He put a hand to his chest on instinct.

The warmth answered, pleased.

Then the tremor came back, angry, like it didn’t like being noticed.

Tiffani saw the motion.

Her eyes narrowed. “Did it—”

Jhace didn’t lie. “For one heartbeat.”

Tiffani wrote without looking down:

**Hunger interaction:** tremor relief noted on chest contact.  
**Potential reinforcement loop.**

Bram exhaled slowly. “That’s not a tool. That’s a trap.”

Corran watched them like he’d been watching this happen in other rooms for too many Tides.

“I’m not here to scare you,” Corran said. “You’re already scared. I’m here because Corlexi is building language. Registry language. Authorization language. Ownership language.”

Tiffani’s voice went hard. “So are we.”

“Yes,” Corran said. “But your language keeps people alive. His language keeps people manageable.”

Bram snorted. “Same difference if you’re the one getting managed.”

Corran didn’t rise to it. “Tallec is filing complaints,” he said.

Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt. “About what.”

“Obstruction,” Corran said. “Interference with production schedules. Deliberate slowdowns. She says your protocols are costing the Crown output.”

Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “We saved two hundred nineteen.”

“Numbers don’t matter when they want control,” Corran said. “Only leverage matters. Tallec gives them leverage. Corlexi gives them structure.”

Bram leaned off the doorframe, shoulders rolling like he was getting ready to fight paperwork with teeth. “So what’s the play, Brother.”

Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. Not friendly. Not hostile.

Tired.

“I shouldn’t be here,” Corran said. “That’s the truth. If Corlexi knew I brought you this packet, I’d be reassigned to somewhere quiet and full of locks.”

“Why do it, then?” Tiffani asked.

Corran hesitated.

That hesitation was the crack in the badge.

“Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of watching people pretend ignorance is the same as innocence.”

Bram let out a short laugh. It wasn’t humor. It was recognition. “Yeah. That’s the city’s favorite lie.”

Corran straightened. “Build your methodology fast,” he said. “Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”

Jhace stared at the packet. At the documented deaths. At the survival cases that weren’t really survival.

He thought of Senne holding until the bell told her to stop.

He thought of Tiffani ringing termination anyway, even when she could see the pocket.

He thought of his own hand going to his sternum and feeling the hunger purr.

Tiffani closed the packet carefully. Not gently.

Carefully, like it was volatile.

“What does Corlexi want?” she asked.

Corran’s expression didn’t change. “Compliance. Names. Locations. Capabilities. Deployment authority through channels.”

“And if we refuse.”

Corran didn’t bother with comfort. “Then unregistered manifestation becomes a category. And categories become crimes.”

Bram’s smile tried to form and failed. “Cool. Saving children is now illegal without a permit.”

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “We finish the ledger.”

Jhace’s hands shook on the bench.

He could taste copper.

He could feel the grid through the floorboards. Doubled hum. New baseline. Predictable enough to plan around.

Predictable enough to weaponize.

“If this registry happens,” Jhace said, voice low, “I’m not holding for paper.”

“I’m not asking you to,” Tiffani said.

Corran moved toward the door.

Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”

Corran paused with his hand on the latch.

“Because I like fewer dead,” he said. “Not because I’m good. Because I’m tired.”

Then he was gone.

The workshop stayed hot.

The tremor stayed.

Tiffani opened her slate to a clean page and wrote a new heading in hard chalk:

**Stage Three: Lock.**

Under it she wrote one line:

**If termination fails, who cuts the hold.**

Jhace watched the words settle into place like a wedge driven home.

His chest heat answered, warm and patient.

Hungry.

---

CHAPTER 6: WITNESS
Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.
The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.
Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.
“For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.
Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.
Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”
“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”
Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”
Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”
Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”
Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”
He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”
Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”
He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.
“Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”
Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”
“Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”
Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”
Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.
“Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”
Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”
Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”
Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”
Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”
“I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”
Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.
Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.
“This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”
Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”
Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”
Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”
Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.
Bram shifted. “You said predators.”
Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”
Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.
Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”
He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”
He turned to leave.
Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”
Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”
Then he was gone.
Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.”
Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.”
Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse.
She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied.
The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate.
She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure.
She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight.
Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down.
And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls.
She took the next step anyway.
She didn’t log it.
Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor.
Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.
---

for idx in range(chap6_start, chap7_start):
    print(f"{idx}: {lines[idx-1]}")

[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS — VARIANT A (REDRAFT, BASE-SCOPE)

---

Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.

The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn’t be missed.

The second was how the man didn’t look tired. Not even a little.

Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.

“For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.

Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.

Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”

“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”

Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”

Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”

Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”

He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”

Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”

Corran didn’t react to the phrasing. If anything, it sounded like he’d heard worse in cleaner rooms.

He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench.

Paper. Old style. Archive weight.

“Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”

Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”

“Enough.” Corran’s voice stayed flat. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”

Jhace stared at the packet like it could bruise him without being opened.

“Why give this to us?” he asked.

Corran’s eyes didn’t leave Jhace’s tremor. “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”

Tiffani’s chin lifted a fraction. “Who.”

Corran hesitated. Not long. Just enough to show he knew what it cost to say.

“Administrator Corlexi,” he said. “Infrastructure Compliance.”

Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Makes you want to wash your own.”

Tiffani opened the packet and read.

Jhace watched her face tighten on certain lines—durations, markers, outcomes. She didn’t dramatize. She didn’t ask for permission to be afraid. She just absorbed.

“This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”

Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”

Jhace’s stomach turned.

Locked hold.

He tasted copper and didn’t know if it was dust or blood memory.

Tiffani’s pen scratched. **Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.**

Bram shifted his weight. “You said predators.”

Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints,” he said. “Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”

Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.

Corran leaned closer, voice lower. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple sermons. I don’t like Guild schedules.” He paused. “But I like fewer dead.”

Tiffani kept reading. Faster now. Like speed could change the endpoint.

“This isn’t just warning,” she said. “This is leverage.”

“Yes,” Corran said. “Corlexi will offer resources. Training. Legal protection for registered manifestors.” His eyes stayed level. “And he’ll take ownership in return.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked harder. “I’m not doing it for paper.”

Corran nodded once, like he’d expected that answer and didn’t consider it heroic. “Then build methodology fast,” he said. “Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”

Tiffani looked up. “Junction Seven.”

Corran’s face didn’t change, but something in his eyes went older.

“Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”

Silence filled the workshop the way dust filled lungs. Slow. Everywhere.

Corran straightened.

He turned to leave.

Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”

Corran paused at the door.

“Because I like fewer dead,” he said. “Not because I’m good. Because I’m tired.”

Then he was gone.

Jhace stared at his hands.

They shook.

---

CHAPTER 6: WITNESS
Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.
The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge. Bright. Clean. Pinned where it couldn’t be missed. The second was how the man didn’t look tired. Not even a little.
Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.
“For the record,” Corran said, “I’m not here to stop you.”
Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”
“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”
Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”
Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”
Bram hovered by the door. “The holy trinity,” he said dry. “Prayer, production, paperwork.”
Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”
He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”
Corran pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.
“Stage Three cases,” he said. “Documented. Not rumors.”
Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”
“Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”
Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”
Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.
“Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”
Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”
Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”
Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”
Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”
“I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”
Tiffani opened the packet and read. Eyes moving fast.
Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.
“This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”
Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”
Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”
Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”
Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.
Bram shifted. “You said predators.”
Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”
Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.
Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”
He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”
He moved toward the door.
Tiffani’s voice stopped him. “Why risk telling us this?”
Corran didn’t turn around. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”
Then he was gone.
Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.”
Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.”
Jhace’s hands shook on the bench.

import re

# find chapter 7 header lines
for i,line in enumerate(text):
    if re.search(r'CHAPTER\s*7', line, re.I):

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc python - <<'PY'
import re
path='/mnt/data/N01Final.Last.txt'
lines=open(path,'r',encoding='utf-8').read().splitlines()
start=506  # 0-index 506 corresponds to line 507
# find next chapter header after start
end=len(lines)
for i in range(start+1,len(lines)):
    if re.match(r'CHAPTER\s*\d+\s*:', lines[i], re.I):
        end=i
        break
print('start',start+1,'end',end)
print('\n'.join(lines[start:end][:2000]))

[TOOL]
start 507 end 611
CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL
Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.
And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume.
It started failing at mid-Tide.
The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.
Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.
Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.
“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”
“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”
Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.
“We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”
“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”
“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”
“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”
She swung her slate toward the operators.
“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”
Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”
Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”
“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.
“Senne—status.”
“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”
“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”
Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”
“Kess—report.”
“I am present and available for assignment.”
“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”
“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”
Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”
“I am not bleeding.”
Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”
“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”
“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”
From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”
Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”
“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”
“Spotters, call markers.”
“Jhace tremor increasing!”
“Corven tremor increasing!”
“Senne vein-line onset!”
Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”
Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”
“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”
Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”
“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”
“One hundred. Out.”
Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”
Jhace’s head snapped. “No.”
“They’re not out.”
“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”
“Enough.”
Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”
“Tiff—” Jhace started.
“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”
Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.
Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”
“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”
Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”
“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.
“Hold.”
Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”
“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”
Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.
“Can’t,” Daved said.
“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.
“Can’t.”
“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”
“They’re not out.”
“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”
Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”
Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”
“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.
“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”
Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”
Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”
Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”
“I see it,” Tiffani said.
They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.
“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.
The light finally cut.
Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.
Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”

They carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.
His eyes were open.
His body was too still.
Tiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.
Tiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.
Her slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.
She had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.
Her hand moved to add the summary line.
She stared at the slate. At the line that read: Casualties: 3.
She tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.
The chalk snapped.
Her hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway.
She stared at her shaking fingers.
She didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.
Some data points weren’t for the ledger.
Some were just for carrying.

---

Jhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.
He'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop.
Method had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.
---

[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL — VARIANT A

---

Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.

And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume.

It started failing at mid-Tide.

The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.

Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.

Tiffani arrived before Jhace.

When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.

“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”

“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”

Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.

“We have a problem,” Merra said. “Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”

“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”

“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”

“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”

She swung her slate toward the operators.

“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”

Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”

Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”

“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.

“Senne—status.”

“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”

“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”

Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”

“Kess—report.”

“I am present and available for assignment.”

“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”

“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”

Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”

“I am not bleeding.”

Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”

“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”

“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”

From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”

Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”

“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”

They moved.

Spotters called markers like curses.

“Jhace tremor increasing!”

“Corven tremor increasing!”

“Senne vein-line onset!”

Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”

Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”

“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”

Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”

“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”

“One hundred,” Jhace said. “Out.”

Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”

Jhace’s head snapped. “No.”

“They’re not out.”

“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”

“Enough.”

Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”

“Tiff—” Jhace started.

“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”

Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.

Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”

“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”

Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”

“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.

“Hold.”

Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”

“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”

Lights guttered and went out—one by one—

Except Daved’s.

“Can’t,” Daved said.

“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.

“Can’t.”

“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”

“They’re not out.”

“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”

Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”

Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”

“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.

“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”

Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”

Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”

“I see it,” Tiffani said.

They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.

“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.

The light finally cut.

Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.

Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”

They carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.

His eyes were open.

His body was too still.

Tiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.

---

Tiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.

Her slate lay open to the taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.

She had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.

Her hand moved to add the summary line.

She stared at the slate. At the line that read: **Casualties: 3.**

She tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.

The chalk snapped.

Her hands shook because she’d been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required.

The chalk snapped anyway.

She stared at her shaking fingers.

She didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring termination at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.

Some data points weren’t for the ledger.

Some were just for carrying.

---

Jhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.

He’d wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result.

Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn’t stop.

Method had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.

---

[USER]
## CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL

Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.

The air smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tried to wear perfume.

The failure started at mid-Tide.

The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.

Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.

Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.

“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”

“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”

Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.

“We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”

“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”

“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”

“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”

She swung her slate toward the operators.

“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”

Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”

Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”

“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.

“Senne—status.”

“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”

“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”

Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”

“Kess—report.”

“I am present and available for assignment.”

“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”

“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”

Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”

“I am not bleeding.”

Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”

“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”

“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”

From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”

Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”

“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”

“Spotters, call markers.”

“Jhace tremor increasing!”

“Corven tremor increasing!”

“Senne vein-line onset!”

Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”

Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”

“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”

Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”

“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”

“One hundred. Out.”

Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”

Jhace’s head snapped. “No.”

“They’re not out.”

“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”

“Enough.”

Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”

“Tiff—” Jhace started.

“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”

Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.

Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”

“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”

Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”

“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.

“Hold.”

Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”

“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”

Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.

“Can’t,” Daved said.

“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.

“Can’t.”

“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”

“They’re not out.”

“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”

Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”

Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”

“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.

“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”

Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”

Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”

“I see it,” Tiffani said.

They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.

“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.

The light finally cut.

Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.

Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”

They carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.

His eyes were open.

His body was too still.

Tiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.

Tiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.

Her slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.

She had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.

Her hand moved to add the summary line.

She stared at the slate. At the line that read: **Casualties: 3**.

She tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.

The chalk snapped.

Her hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway.

She stared at her shaking fingers.

She didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.

Some data points weren’t for the ledger.

Some were just for carrying.

Jhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.

He'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop.

Method had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.

[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES — VARIANT A

---

Daved didn’t wake like people woke.

He came back in pieces.

A blink that didn’t track. A breath that hitched and then forgot what it was doing. Fingers that twitched once, like the body was testing whether it still owned them.

Jhace stood at the foot of the cot and watched all of it happen.

The room wasn’t a ward. It was a repurposed counting office behind Merchant Hall. Stone walls. Iron brackets. Two rows of cots shoved too close together because “space” was a privilege the city had run out of.

It smelled like sweat and antiseptic resin and copper you couldn’t wash off.

Tiffani sat on an overturned stool with her slate on her knees.

Not writing.

Just holding it there like if she put it down the numbers would crawl off the page and start screaming.

Bram paced near the doorway, hands in his hair, then hands at his sides, then hands back in his hair. Like he couldn’t decide what posture made him less useless.

A medic leaned over Daved with a lamp and clean cloth. Not Temple. Not Crown. Just a worker with practiced hands and tired eyes.

“His jaw’s locked,” the medic said.

“He was locked,” Bram muttered, like saying it softer would change what it meant.

The medic checked Daved’s pulse with two fingers. Counted under their breath.

“Heart’s irregular,” they said. “Breath is shallow. He’s conscious enough to be miserable. Not conscious enough to answer.”

Jhace’s hands shook at his sides.

Fine vibration. Constant. The kind you could pretend was cold until you tried to hold a tool and it proved you wrong.

He tasted copper.

Not flood. Not activation. Just the low tang that had taken up residence since the Piercing and refused to pay rent.

“How long,” Jhace said, and hated how small his voice came out, “does this last.”

The medic’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s tremor, then away like it was impolite to stare at the obvious.

“Three Tides,” they said. “If he’s going to stabilize without secondary failure. If he’s going to breathe on his own without assistance. If his hands are going to remember how to be hands.”

“Three Tides,” Bram echoed. “That’s… manageable. That’s a number.”

The medic didn’t smile. “It’s a checkpoint. Not a promise.”

Tiffani finally looked up. Her face was dust-streaked and blank in the way blankness becomes when it’s holding something back.

“Stage Three,” she said.

The medic nodded once. “Locked hold is a body event. Not just a light event. People talk like it’s magic. It’s not. It’s stress. It’s collapse inside the ribs.”

Jhace stared at Daved’s hands.

They weren’t shaking.

They were still.

Stillness can be worse than tremor. Tremor means you’re still trying.

Daved’s fingers lay curled into the sheet like he’d been told to hold and his body had obeyed too well.

“He saved them,” Bram said, too fast. Too bright. “He held the last span. He did what we asked.”

“No,” Tiffani said.

Her voice cut the room clean. Not cruelty. Correction.

“We didn’t ask,” she said. “We ordered. And he didn’t stop.”

Bram’s mouth tightened. “You want to assign blame, do it later.”

“I’m assigning mechanism,” Tiffani said. “If we can’t say what happened without comforting ourselves, we can’t prevent it.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked harder.

He wanted to tell her to shut up. Wanted to tell her the ledger could wait. Wanted to tell her Daved was still warm and that meant they were allowed to hope.

Instead he said, “What do we do.”

The medic straightened. “We keep him warm. We keep him breathing. We keep him from trying to hold again.”

Bram barked a laugh that died halfway. “Good luck. The bells don’t care about doctor orders.”

The medic looked at Bram like Bram was a child who kept licking hot iron.

“Then you make it care,” the medic said. “You pull him out of rotation. You put someone else in. You live with that.”

Jhace felt something cold settle in his gut.

Someone else.

Another name.

Another set of hands that would shake forever.

Tiffani’s pen started moving again.

Jhace didn’t look at the slate. He could feel the words forming anyway.

**Daved. Locked. Removed from rotation.**

It didn’t matter if she wrote it. The city would write it in him.

---

On the second Tide, Daved could speak.

Barely.

The medic propped him up against rolled cloth so he wouldn’t choke on his own spit. Daved’s eyes were open now. Flat. Not calm.

Just empty of surprise.

Jhace stood close enough to hear without leaning in, because leaning in felt like begging.

Daved’s throat worked. “Did—”

Jhace answered before the question finished. “They got out.”

Daved blinked once. Slow.

“Good,” he rasped.

His voice sounded like dust dragged over stone.

Tiffani stood at the foot of the cot, slate in hand, face controlled.

“You locked,” she said.

Daved’s eyes moved to her. “I know.”

“Termination failed,” she said.

Daved’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. Not even bitterness. Just recognition.

“Termination didn’t matter,” he said. “My hands didn’t listen.”

Jhace’s tremor surged up his forearm like it wanted to crawl into his shoulder again.

Daved’s gaze flicked to Jhace’s shaking fingers.

“You’re next,” Daved said, voice flat.

Jhace swallowed copper. “Don’t.”

“It’s not a threat,” Daved whispered. “It’s math.”

Tiffani’s pen scratched.

Jhace watched her write and hated it and needed it.

Daved’s eyes closed for two heartbeats. Opened again.

“How many dead,” he asked.

Tiffani didn’t answer.

Bram did, because Bram couldn’t stand silence when silence meant guilt.

“Three,” Bram said. “Stairwell. Route failure. Not you.”

Daved’s eyes stayed on the ceiling. “Still three.”

Jhace breathed out through his nose and tasted copper again, sharper this time.

Not activation.

Just the world reminding him it had teeth.

---

On the third Tide, Daved tried to sit up without help.

The attempt looked pathetic and heroic at the same time.

His abdominal muscles engaged like they were moving through mud. His shoulders shook. His breath went thin.

He got halfway up before his arms gave out.

Jhace stepped forward on instinct to catch him.

Daved’s hand shot out and grabbed Jhace’s sleeve.

Not steady. Not clean.

But a grip.

And it shook.

Stage One. Or whatever came after Stage Three when the body crawled back from the edge and found it had been changed.

Daved stared at his own hand like it was an animal he didn’t trust.

“It’s back,” he said.

Jhace nodded once. “Yeah.”

Daved’s jaw tightened. “I can’t feel my fingers right.”

The medic stepped in, checked the grip, checked the skin color, checked the tremor amplitude with eyes trained to read small failures.

“You can feel them enough,” the medic said. “To know they aren’t yours the way they used to be.”

Daved’s breath came out in a short, ugly sound. “I can’t—”

“You can,” the medic said. “Just not what you want.”

Jhace watched Daved’s hand tremble against his sleeve.

He pictured Daved trying to hold a spoon.

Trying to tie a collar knot.

Trying to fasten his own coat.

He pictured Daved waking every Tide and negotiating with his hands like they were a hostile crew.

The medic touched Daved’s wrist. “No holds,” they said. “Doctor’s order.”

Daved laughed once. It turned into a cough.

“You write that on a bell?” he rasped.

Bram’s voice came rough. “We’ll write it on your face if we have to.”

Tiffani didn’t smile.

She wrote: **Removed from rotation. Non-negotiable.**

Jhace looked at her. “You think writing it makes it true.”

“I think writing it makes it enforceable,” Tiffani said.

“By who.”

Tiffani’s eyes didn’t move. “By us.”

Bram swallowed. “Until someone with a seal decides ‘us’ isn’t official.”

Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we’ll be unofficial and alive.”

Jhace tasted copper and felt the tremor in his hands pulse like a heartbeat.

Daved watched him.

“Don’t try to be clean about it,” Daved said, quiet. “It’s not clean.”

Jhace nodded once.

He didn’t trust his voice.

He stared at his hands until the shaking felt like a language he would have to learn.

Three Tides.

Checkpoint. Not promise.

Outside the counting office, the city hummed its doubled rhythm and pretended that was stability.

Jhace’s hands shook anyway.

---

[USER]
locking this version
CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES
Daved didn't wake like people woke.
He came back in pieces.
The room wasn't a ward. It was a repurposed counting office behind Merchant Hall. Stone walls. Iron brackets. Two rows of cots shoved too close together because "space" was a privilege the city had run out of.
It smelled like sweat and antiseptic resin and copper you couldn't wash off.
Tide one: the eyes opened.
Not together. Left first. Then right. Like the body was testing whether it still owned them.
A medic leaned over the cot with a lamp and clean cloth. Not Temple. Not Crown. Just a worker with practiced hands and tired eyes.
The light caught Daved's pupils. They contracted. Uneven. The left dragged.
Jhace stood at the foot of the cot with his hands jammed in his pockets because if he left them out they shook hard enough to rattle the tools on his belt.
Tiffani sat on an overturned stool with her slate on her knees.
Not writing yet.
Just holding it there like if she put it down the numbers would crawl off the page and start screaming.
Bram paced near the doorway, hands in his hair, then hands at his sides, then hands back in his hair. Like he couldn't decide what posture made him less useless.
Daved's mouth moved. No sound. Just the jaw working like it remembered words but couldn't find the mechanism.
The medic set the lamp down. "Response uneven. No voice yet. We wait."
Jhace's hands shook at his sides.
Fine vibration. Constant. The kind you could pretend was cold until you tried to hold a tool and it proved you wrong.
He tasted copper.
Not flood. Not activation. Just the low tang that had taken up residence since the Piercing and refused to pay rent.
"How long," Jhace said, and hated how small his voice came out, "does this last."
The medic's eyes flicked to Jhace's tremor, then away like it was impolite to stare at the obvious.
"Three Tides," they said. "If he's going to stabilize without secondary failure. If he's going to breathe on his own without assistance. If his hands are going to remember how to be hands."
"Three Tides," Bram echoed. "That's… manageable. That's a number."
The medic didn't smile. "It's a checkpoint. Not a promise."
Tiffani finally looked up. Her face was dust-streaked and blank in the way blankness becomes when it's holding something back.
"Stage Three," she said.
The medic nodded once. "Locked hold is a body event. Not just a light event. People talk like it's magic. It's not. It's stress. It's collapse inside the ribs."
Tiffani's pen moved across the slate. Tide one: ocular response present. Vocal absent. Motor asymmetry noted.
Jhace stared at Daved's hands.
They weren't shaking.
They were still.
Stillness can be worse than tremor. Tremor means you're still trying.
Daved's fingers lay curled into the sheet like he'd been told to hold and his body had obeyed too well.
Tide two: the voice arrived.
Not clean. Dust dragged over stone.
The medic was there with water in a tin cup. He held it to Daved's mouth. Daved swallowed. Choked once. Swallowed again.
Daved's throat worked. "Did—"
Jhace answered before the question finished. "They got out."
Daved blinked once. Slow.
"Good," he rasped.
His voice sounded like dust dragged over stone.
Tiffani stood at the foot of the cot, slate in hand, face controlled.
"You locked," she said.
Daved's eyes moved to her. "I know."
"Termination failed," she said.
Daved's mouth twitched. Not a smile. Not even bitterness. Just recognition.
"Termination didn't matter," he said. "My hands didn't listen."
Jhace's tremor surged up his forearm like it wanted to crawl into his shoulder again.
Daved's gaze flicked to Jhace's shaking fingers.
"You're next," Daved said, voice flat.
Jhace swallowed copper. "Don't."
"It's not a threat," Daved whispered. "It's math."
Tiffani's pen scratched across her slate. Tide two: vocal response partial. Operator projection: Jhace Torrins. Accumulation risk high.
Jhace watched her write and hated it and needed it.
Daved's eyes closed for two heartbeats. Opened again.
"How many dead," he asked.
Tiffani didn't answer.
Bram did, because Bram couldn't stand silence when silence meant guilt.
"Three," Bram said. "Stairwell. Route failure. Not you."
Daved's eyes stayed on the ceiling. "Still three."
Jhace breathed out through his nose and tasted copper again, sharper this time.
Not activation.
Just the world reminding him it had teeth.
Tide three: the body tried.
Daved tried to sit up without help.
The attempt looked pathetic and heroic at the same time.
His abdominal muscles engaged like they were moving through mud. His shoulders shook. His breath went thin.
He got halfway up before his arms gave out.
Jhace stepped forward on instinct to catch him.
Daved's hand shot out and grabbed Jhace's sleeve.
Not steady. Not clean.
But a grip.
And it shook.
Stage One. Or whatever came after Stage Three when the body crawled back from the edge and found it had been changed.
Daved stared at his own hand like it was an animal he didn't trust.
"It's back," he said.
Jhace nodded once. "Yeah."
Daved's jaw tightened. "I can't feel my fingers right."
The medic stepped in, checked the grip, checked the skin color, checked the tremor amplitude with eyes trained to read small failures.
"You can feel them enough," the medic said. "To know they aren't yours the way they used to be."
Daved's breath came out in a short, ugly sound. "I can't—"
"You can," the medic said. "Just not what you want."
Jhace watched Daved's hand tremble against his sleeve.
He pictured Daved trying to hold a spoon.
Trying to tie a collar knot.
Trying to fasten his own coat.
He pictured Daved waking every Tide and negotiating with his hands like they were a hostile crew.
The medic touched Daved's wrist. "No holds," they said. "Doctor's order."
Daved laughed once. It turned into a cough.
"You write that on a bell?" he rasped.
Bram's voice came rough. "We'll write it on your face if we have to."
Tiffani didn't smile.
She wrote: Tide three: motor attempt partial. Tremor onset. Removed from rotation. Non-negotiable.
Jhace looked at her. "You think writing it makes it true."
"I think writing it makes it enforceable," Tiffani said.
"By who."
Tiffani's eyes didn't move. "By us."
Bram swallowed. "Until someone with a seal decides 'us' isn't official."
Tiffani's mouth went flat. "Then we'll be unofficial and alive."
Jhace tasted copper and felt the tremor in his hands pulse like a heartbeat.
Daved watched him.
"Don't try to be clean about it," Daved said, quiet. "It's not clean."
Jhace nodded once.
He didn't trust his voice.
Bram shifted against the doorframe. "This room still smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne."
"Leave," Tiffani said, without heat.
"I'm behaving," Bram said. "I'm just… shaped wrong."
Daved rasped quietly. "You okay."
Tiffani's pen stopped.
"Define operational parameters for 'okay.'"
"Not irrelevant."
"Compared to your autonomic failure, my current symptoms are statistically minor."
"Still matters."
Tiffani swallowed once. "I can't get the copper taste out of my mouth anymore. Food tastes wrong. Water tastes wrong. I think it's in my teeth."
Jhace started, "Tiff—"
"That's not a Stage marker," she cut in. "So it doesn't get logged."
Daved spoke slow. "Don't let them call this holy."
"I won't."
"Nobody's calling this holy," Bram said. "They're calling it 'resource allocation.' Much sexier."
"Bram."
"I know," he said. "I'm disgusting. It's my brand."
Jhace didn't know if that was a promise to Daved or a threat to the world.
He stared at his hands until the shaking felt like a language he would have to learn.
Three Tides.
Checkpoint. Not promise.
Outside the counting office, the city hummed its doubled rhythm and pretended that was stability.
Jhace's hands shook anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
Chapter 8 is now **LOCKED** exactly as pasted.

Locked set is now: **Chapters 1–8**.

Canon additions from your locked Ch8 that are now binding going forward:
- **Three-Tide recovery frame** is explicit and structured (Tide one eyes, Tide two voice, Tide three motor attempt).
- Daved’s post-lock return includes **tremor onset** on Tide three + **removed from rotation, non-negotiable**.
- Tiffani admits the **copper taste persistence** (in teeth; food/water wrong) and explicitly refuses to log it because it’s “not a Stage marker.”
- Bram’s “piss and heroism” / “shaped wrong” banter is canon tone for aftermath rooms.

draft 9

[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 9: SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR) — VARIANT A

---

Rumor moved faster than bells.

It didn’t need cadence. It didn’t need proof. It just needed mouths.

By the second Tide after Daved’s eyes started tracking, the Underface was already trading a new story.

Not about holds.

Not about tremor.

About someone who didn’t need to touch the structure at all.

Jhace heard it first from a runner who’d come for wedge slips and left with a mouthful of panic.

“Someone cut a beam,” the runner said. “Not sawed. Not wedged. Cut.”

Jhace kept his hands jammed in his pockets. The tremor made the fabric buzz against his knuckles.

“Cut how.”

The runner shrugged too hard. “Like it was soft.”

Bram leaned against the doorway, half a grin loaded and failing to fire. “People cut beams all the time.”

“Not like this,” the runner said. “No sparks. No tools. Just—” He made a straight line with his hand through the air. “And it opened.”

Tiffani didn’t look up from her slate. “Where.”

“Sector Twelve,” the runner said. “Near the spill-alleys. No bell. Just screaming.”

“No bell,” Tiffani repeated.

“Yeah. Because nothing collapsed. It just… stopped being connected.”

Jhace tasted copper.

Not flood. Not activation.

Just the baseline tang that now lived behind every word.

“Who saw it,” Tiffani said.

“Two guards. One dockhand. An old woman who never lies because she doesn’t think anyone’s worth lying to.” The runner swallowed. “They said it was a person. Not light. Not hold. A person.”

Bram’s voice went dry. “People are always the worst part.”

The runner looked at Bram like he didn’t know whether to laugh or throw up. “They said the person was carrying something.”

Tiffani’s pen finally paused.

“Describe,” she said.

The runner made a shape with his hands like he was trying to hold the air without getting cut.

“A piece. Long. Flat. Like metal but not metal. No handle. Just… edge.”

Jhace’s chest went tight.

He didn’t know why that detail hit harder than “cut a beam.”

He knew exactly why.

The spindle. The copper-gold particulate. The grit that tingled on skin.

Pieces had to be somewhere.

Pieces didn’t stay harmless just because they were smaller.

Tiffani wrote a new line in hard chalk.

**Rumor:** non-contact structural severance.  
**Object:** shard fragment (blade-form).  
**Location:** Sector Twelve spill-alleys.  
**No bell. No collapse.**

Bram watched her write. “You’re documenting rumors now.”

“I’m documenting inputs,” Tiffani said. “Rumors become bell calls when they get bored.”

Jhace stared at his hands.

They shook anyway.

---

They didn’t go to Sector Twelve as a team.

Not officially.

Official meant eyes. Badges. Questions. Corlexi’s clean hands writing their names into a new category.

So they went like workers.

Bram first, because Bram could walk into a crowd and come out with the right lie.

Tiffani second, because she could hear patterns in nonsense and call it evidence.

Jhace last, because Jhace had become recognizable in the wrong way.

They moved through mid-terrace markets where the doubled hum made glass rattle in frames. Copper-gold grit clung to iron hooks and nails like it had preferences.

The city looked stable from a distance.

Up close, every seam was one breath away from deciding otherwise.

Bram found the first witness at a stall that sold boiled root and cheap cloth.

Old woman. Bent spine. Hands like dried rope.

She didn’t look impressed when Bram said hello. She looked annoyed that he had used air.

“You’re asking about the cut,” she said.

Bram blinked. “I didn’t even—”

“I hear you,” she said. “You ask the same way everyone asks. Like you’re trying to look casual while your eyes scream.”

Tiffani stepped in. “Tell me what you saw.”

The woman’s gaze flicked to Tiffani’s slate. “You write it down.”

“Yes.”

“Good. Then you can’t pretend later.” The woman spit into the dust. “I saw a woman walk down the alley like she owned the angle of it. Didn’t run. Didn’t hurry. Just walked.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened.

“Carrying something?” Tiffani asked.

The woman nodded. “A piece. Long. Thin. No handle. Like a blade that never learned how to be a tool.”

Bram made a face. “That’s poetic for an old woman selling root.”

The woman’s eyes sharpened. “I can still hate prettily.”

Tiffani didn’t flinch. “What did she do with it.”

The woman lifted her hand and made the same straight line the runner had made.

“She didn’t touch the beam. She pointed the piece at it. Like she was accusing it.”

Jhace felt his teeth itch.

The doubled hum under the city grid did that when his body was already listening.

“And then?” Tiffani asked.

“And then the beam wasn’t one beam anymore.” The woman’s voice stayed flat. “It was two. The top part sagged and settled like it had been waiting for permission. Nobody died because nobody was under it. The screaming was because people saw it happen.”

Bram exhaled. “So the rumor’s true.”

“Truth doesn’t matter,” the woman said. “Fear does. Fear spreads. Truth just trails behind, trying to keep up.”

Tiffani wrote.

Jhace watched the chalk move and felt that old anger rise—the one that wasn’t at Corlexi or the Temple or the Guild.

At the world.

At how fast it broke.

“How close were you,” Tiffani asked.

“Close enough to smell her.” The woman grimaced. “Not sweat. Not blood. Copper. Like she’d been chewing coins.”

Jhace swallowed.

He could taste copper right now.

Always.

But the way the woman said it made it sound different.

Not baseline.

Imprinted.

Tiffani’s pen scratched faster.

**Sensory:** copper odor on subject.  
**Method:** non-contact severance using shard fragment.  
**Effect:** clean split. No collapse initiated.

Bram shifted. “Did she have tremor.”

The woman barked a short laugh. “You think people who do that stand there shaking like a sick dog?”

Jhace’s jaw clenched.

Tiffani didn’t look up. “Did she shake.”

“No,” the woman said. “Her hand was steady. Steady like she didn’t care if it worked because it had already worked before.”

Bram’s voice went quiet. “That’s not good.”

Tiffani closed her slate.

“We don’t approach,” she said.

Bram frowned. “We came all this way to not approach?”

“We came to confirm the rumor is real,” Tiffani said. “It’s real.”

Jhace felt the coal-warmth behind his sternum stir—just a shift, like it didn’t like being told no to anything in the world.

He kept his hands in his pockets.

He didn’t touch his chest.

He didn’t give it a reason.

“Corlexi will hear,” Bram said.

“Everyone will hear,” Tiffani said. “And they’ll ask the same questions they always ask.”

“Is it divine,” Bram said.

“Is it labor,” Tiffani said.

“Is it property,” Jhace finished.

Tiffani looked at him. “And the worst one.”

Jhace’s mouth went tight. “Is it controllable.”

---

They didn’t see her.

Not directly.

But the alley held evidence the way a body held bruises.

A beam cut too clean to be fracture. Two halves that didn’t splinter. No saw marks. No wedge scars. No strap abrasion.

Just absence where connection used to be.

Tiffani crouched and ran a fingertip near the cut line without touching it.

“Smooth,” she said.

Bram leaned close. “Don’t touch it.”

“I’m not,” Tiffani said. “I’m looking.”

Jhace stared at the cut like it had insulted him personally.

His holds were desperate bracing. Ugly. Contact. Tremor. Blood.

This was neat.

Neat was dangerous. Neat was seductive. Neat looked like control.

Jhace could feel the doubled hum in his molars.

It wasn’t louder here.

It was sharper.

As if something had resonated in this alley and left the air slightly changed.

Bram rubbed his palms on his coat. “So what do we do.”

Tiffani stood slowly. Her knee caught for half a heartbeat. She ignored it.

“We add a new category,” she said.

Bram’s brow lifted. “To your taxonomy.”

“Not mine,” Tiffani said. “Ours. And we keep it separate.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked.

“Separate how,” Jhace asked.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed hard. “We do not put this in the operator ledger. Not yet. Not until we know what it costs. Not until we know what it does to people.”

Bram snorted. “Too late. It’s already doing.”

Tiffani didn’t deny it. “Then we keep it from becoming the Crown’s excuse.”

“Excuse for what?” Bram asked.

“For taking us,” Jhace said, before Tiffani could. “For saying ‘look what happens when it’s unregistered.’”

Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Exactly.”

Bram stared down the alley like he expected the shard-bearer to walk back through it just to prove a point.

“She’s a person,” Bram said. “Not a bell. Not a building. A person walking around with a piece of the Piercing like it’s a knife.”

Jhace breathed in.

Copper.

Always copper.

He didn’t like how much the idea made his skin feel tight.

Not fear.

Recognition.

Because if there was a way to cut without shaking, there would be people lining up to pay for it.

And people lining up to own it.

---

That Tide, late, when the city quieted into its doubled baseline and the bells finally shut up long enough for sleep to pretend it existed—

someone walked the spill-alleys again.

No badge.

No crew.

No slate.

A woman with a long flat piece wrapped in cloth like it was a wound that refused to heal.

She stopped under a lantern with failing oil.

Unwrapped the cloth.

The shard caught the light and didn’t reflect it the way metal did.

It drank light. Bent it. Made the air around it look thin.

Her hand didn’t shake.

Her mouth tasted copper so constantly she’d stopped noticing.

She held the shard up like a question.

Not to the sky.

To the city.

To the seams.

To the parts that still pretended they were stable.

She didn’t want to collapse buildings.

She wanted something else.

Something smaller.

Personal.

She listened to the doubled hum the way a musician listened to a pitch, except she didn’t have words for it. She only had feeling.

Wrongness.

Alignment.

A place to cut.

Her fingers tightened.

Not in panic. In choice.

And somewhere in the terrace above, iron screamed once—quiet, brief—as a single bracket failed cleanly and dropped without taking anything else with it.

A controlled failure.

A message.

She wrapped the shard again and walked on.

---

Back in the counting office, Daved slept with his hands curled into the sheet.

Jhace sat on a crate beside the cot, watching the tremor in his own fingers like it was a language he hadn’t agreed to learn.

Tiffani stood in the doorway, slate tucked under her arm, staring out at the dark streets where rumors moved.

Bram leaned on the opposite wall, too awake, too loud even when he was quiet.

“We have a shard-bearer,” Bram said.

“We have a rumor,” Tiffani corrected.

Jhace didn’t look up. “It’s real.”

Tiffani’s eyes didn’t move. “Then we treat it like a separate problem.”

Bram let out a slow breath. “Yeah.”

Jhace finally lifted his hands out of his pockets.

They shook.

Not because of cold.

Because the city had taught his body a new baseline.

He stared at the tremor and wondered what it would feel like to be steady.

Outside, the doubled hum ran through stone and iron like an invitation nobody had asked for.

And somewhere in Sector Twelve, a clean edge waited for the next seam.

---
