[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## CHAPTER 10: COMPLIANCE

---

The summons came on cheap paper with an expensive seal.

A runner dropped it on Jhace’s bench like it might bite. No bell. No warning cadence. Just a stamped crest and a requirement pretending to be polite.

Tiffani didn’t touch it at first.

Jhace didn’t either.

His hands shook hard enough that the bolt tray rattled. He shoved both hands into his pockets and made himself stand still.

Bram read the seal upside down and still understood it. “Infrastructure Compliance,” he said. “That’s the part of the Crown that smells like soap and threatens you with grammar.”

Tiffani finally picked it up with two fingers. Broke the seal clean. Unfolded the sheet.

Her eyes moved fast.

Her mouth went flat.

“Administrator Corlexi,” she said.

Jhace tasted copper.

Not flood. Not activation. Just that baseline tang that lived in his teeth now and flared whenever someone said a name that could put him in a box.

“What does it say,” Jhace asked.

Tiffani held the page so the light hit it clean.

**REPORT IMMEDIATELY.**  
**SUBJECT: OPERATIONAL CAPABILITY / PUBLIC SAFETY.**  
**DELIVER: ALL NOTES, LEDGERS, AND PROTOCOL DOCUMENTATION.**  
**FAILURE TO COMPLY WILL BE RECORDED.**

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Recorded.”

Bram snorted. “They love that word. Makes it sound like you’re a weather pattern and not a person.”

Tiffani folded the summons once. Then again. Precise creases. Like if she kept it orderly, it couldn’t become a weapon.

“They want the ledger,” she said.

Jhace stared at the slate wall where Stage One and Stage Two rules lived in chalk. Spotters required. Duration caps. Termination authority.

The words looked thin against what the Crown wanted.

“They’re not getting it,” Jhace said.

Tiffani didn’t argue the sentiment. She argued the math.

“If we don’t go,” she said, “they come here.”

Bram tilted his head. “Or they come with smiles and say you’re ‘invited’ and the invitation has guards attached.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked harder, like his body wanted to answer the threat with action.

He forced his breath slow.

“One Tide,” Bram said, watching him. “Can you buy one Tide by going and not handing them your spine?”

Tiffani’s pen was already moving. Not on the ledger. On a separate scrap slate—small, pocket size.

“What are you writing,” Jhace asked.

“Terms,” she said. “Boundaries. What we give. What we don’t.”

Bram leaned over her shoulder. “You’re negotiating with a seal.”

“I’m negotiating with a person holding the seal,” Tiffani said. “Different problem.”

Jhace swallowed copper and nodded once. “Then we go.”

---

The Compliance building sat where stone met polish.

Mid-terrace, not deep enough to smell like the Underface, not high enough to pretend it was holy. It had clean steps and iron rails that didn’t rattle. Windows with intact glass. A door that closed flush instead of arguing with its frame.

Inside, the doubled hum was quieter.

Not gone.

Managed.

Jhace hated that most.

They took his collar keys at the entry desk. Took Bram’s strap knife. Took Tiffani’s lamp.

They tried to take her slate.

Tiffani kept her hand on it. “No.”

The clerk blinked like he’d never heard that sound in his life.

“Protocol documentation is listed as deliverable,” he said, voice practiced.

“This is not deliverable,” Tiffani said. “This is my body’s memory.”

Bram leaned in with a grin that was all teeth and no warmth. “And if you try to take it, you’re going to learn what ‘deliverable’ looks like when it hits you in the face.”

Jhace didn’t smile. He watched the clerk’s eyes flick to Jhace’s hands, then away too fast.

Even here, the tremor was visible.

They were escorted down a corridor that smelled like ink and resin. No sweat. No dust. No copper-gold grit caught in cracks.

Jhace’s boots sounded too loud on the stone.

He didn’t like the way the building made him feel like a mistake walking through someone else’s order.

A door opened.

Administrator Corlexi stood behind a desk that looked built to outlive him.

Clean hands. Clean coat. Clean collar.

Not a speck of dust on him anywhere. Like he existed in a separate air supply.

His eyes went to Jhace’s hands immediately.

Not to pity. Not to fear.

To inventory.

“Jhace Torrins,” Corlexi said. “Inspector marks verified.”

Then his gaze shifted to Tiffani.

“Tiffani Merrow,” he said. “Glass-House trained. Unregistered field analyst.”

“Unregistered,” Bram echoed, under his breath. “There it is.”

Corlexi didn’t look at Bram. He didn’t have to. Bram was noise. Corlexi dealt in categories.

“You have been responding to bell failures,” Corlexi said. “You have manifested structural stabilization behavior. You have documented protocols. And you have, despite informal status, influenced evacuation outcomes across multiple sectors.”

Tiffani set her slate on the desk. Not offered. Not surrendered. Placed.

“We’ve kept people alive,” she said.

Corlexi nodded once, like he was acknowledging a number on a ledger. “Public safety is the Crown’s mandate.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked. “Funny. I filed a report about Junction Seven for two Castings and nobody cared.”

Corlexi’s expression didn’t change. “We are not discussing historic negligence. We are discussing present capability.”

Bram leaned back against the wall. “Translation: he doesn’t want to talk about how the city likes dead workers as long as the paperwork’s tidy.”

Corlexi’s eyes finally touched Bram. Brief. Flat. Dismissive.

“Mr. Kerris,” Corlexi said. “Your record is… extensive.”

Bram smiled. “I’m memorable.”

Corlexi looked back to Jhace. “I have read witness statements describing you holding a failing structure without mechanical bracing.”

Jhace didn’t deny it.

“Witness statements also describe tremor onset,” Corlexi continued, “and post-termination impairment. This suggests limited deployability.”

Tiffani’s pen scratched once. A single line. **Deployability** underlined.

Corlexi watched her write.

Then he extended his hand toward the slate, palm up.

“Protocol documentation,” he said.

Tiffani didn’t move. “No.”

The word landed hard in the clean room.

Corlexi’s brow lifted a fraction. A controlled surprise.

“You misunderstand,” he said. “This is not a request.”

Tiffani’s voice stayed level. “You misunderstand. This is not yours.”

Corlexi’s gaze sharpened. “Those notes constitute public infrastructure intelligence.”

“Those notes constitute survival,” Tiffani said. “If you take them, you will turn them into compliance language and get people killed following a script.”

Corlexi’s mouth tightened. “Order prevents panic.”

“Order prevents liability,” Bram muttered. “Panic prevents funerals.”

Jhace’s hands shook in his pockets like they wanted out.

The warmth behind his sternum stirred—banked heat shifting in response to stress.

Jhace forced it down.

Corlexi leaned forward slightly. “You will be registered. Your deployments will be authorized. Your durations will be capped. Your terminations will be supervised.”

Tiffani’s pen moved again. **Supervised termination.** She didn’t like the phrase. She wrote it anyway.

“And if we refuse,” Jhace asked.

Corlexi’s voice didn’t change. “Then you become uncontrolled variables. Variables are contained.”

The word *contained* hit Jhace like a collar.

A side door opened without a knock.

Tallec stepped in.

Dust on her boots, unlike everyone else in the room. Her hands were gloved. Her posture carried the kind of authority that didn’t bother explaining itself.

She looked at Jhace’s tremor like it offended her.

“You’re slowing production,” she said to Bram, not bothering with greeting. “Your protocols are interfering with schedules.”

Tiffani’s eyes flicked once. Not to Tallec’s face. To her hands.

Inventory. Loss. Missing pieces.

Corlexi didn’t glance at Tallec. She was an instrument, not a partner.

“Tallec,” Corlexi said, “observe only.”

Tallec’s jaw tightened. She didn’t like being told to observe.

“Merchant Hall cost three lives,” Tallec said, loud enough to make it a weapon. “Your ‘method’ did not prevent that.”

Tiffani didn’t flinch. “Our method limited it.”

Tallec’s eyes narrowed. “Limited. Convenient word.”

Jhace tasted copper. His hands shook harder.

Corlexi raised one clean hand. Not to stop Tallec. To reassert control.

“The Crown is offering structure,” he said. “Resources. Legal protection. Medical access for your… condition.” His eyes flicked to Jhace’s hands again. “In exchange for accountability.”

Bram laughed once, sharp. “He said ‘offering’ like it isn’t a leash.”

Corlexi ignored him. “Your ledger will be copied.”

Tiffani’s fingers tightened on the slate edge. “No.”

Corlexi’s calm cracked by a hair. “Miss Merrow—”

“No,” she repeated, and this time there was heat under it. “We will report outcomes. Durations. Casualties. We will not surrender full protocol development to a seal.”

Corlexi held her gaze.

Measured.

Then he leaned back and nodded as if he’d predicted this resistance.

“Very well,” he said. “Partial compliance.”

Bram blinked. “He’s bargaining?”

Corlexi’s eyes stayed on Tiffani. “Daily reporting. All deployments logged through Compliance. Operator roster submitted each Tide. Witness verification attached.”

Jhace’s jaw clenched. “That’s still a registry.”

“It is,” Corlexi said. “And it is inevitable.”

Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “And if we refuse partial.”

Corlexi’s voice stayed clean. “Then I authorize containment.”

Tallec’s gloved hand shifted, almost eager.

Jhace’s chest heat flared. His fingers twitched in his pockets like they wanted to clamp onto the nearest beam and make this room stop existing.

He didn’t.

He breathed.

He held his body like he held a structure—minimum motion, maximum intent.

Tiffani stared at Corlexi for three heartbeats, then nodded once.

“Daily reporting,” she said. “Outcomes only. No protocol pages. No operator termination technique detail.”

Corlexi smiled without warmth. “Agreed.”

Bram’s voice went low. “You just signed a deal with the devil’s filing cabinet.”

Tiffani didn’t look at him. “I bought us time.”

Corlexi slid a fresh sheet across the desk. New seal. New category.

“Sign,” he said.

Jhace didn’t move.

Tiffani didn’t move.

Bram laughed softly. “Oh, he wants signatures. That’s adorable.”

Corlexi’s eyes narrowed. “This is not optional.”

Jhace pulled his shaking hands from his pockets and put them flat on the desk.

The tremor made his fingertips tick against the stone.

He stared at his own hands like they were an enemy he’d been forced to bring into the room.

“I’m not doing it for paper,” Jhace said, voice rough. “I’ll report because people die when we don’t. But you don’t own my hands.”

Corlexi looked at the tremor.

Then he looked up at Jhace’s face.

“Ownership is not the question,” Corlexi said. “Control is.”

Jhace tasted copper.

Tiffani signed.

Not with flourish. Not with surrender. Just a controlled mark on a controlled line.

Then she slid the page back like she’d shoved a blade across a table.

Corlexi took it. “You will be contacted for your first scheduled briefing,” he said.

Bram pushed off the wall. “Congrats. You’re official enough to be watched.”

They were escorted out.

Back into the corridor. Back toward dust and noise and real air.

The moment Jhace stepped outside, the doubled hum hit him louder again—raw through the stone, unfiltered.

He hated how relieved he was to hear it.

Tiffani didn’t speak until they were two streets away.

“Daily reporting buys us a Tide,” she said. “Maybe two.”

“And then,” Bram asked.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed forward. “Then they tighten.”

Jhace’s hands shook at his sides.

He didn’t hide them.

He stared at the tremor and tried to imagine what “control” would look like when it wasn’t his.

Somewhere down-terrace, a bell rang.

Not frantic yet.

Warning cadence.

A structure beginning to argue with gravity.

Jhace tasted copper and felt the banked heat behind his sternum answer like it had been waiting.

Bram watched his hands. “Don’t go clean about it,” he said, almost mocking, almost kind. “It’s not clean.”

Jhace didn’t trust his voice.

He just started walking toward the bell, hands shaking in the open air, while the city hummed its doubled rhythm like it didn’t care who had the seal.

yeah you did too much....
## CHAPTER 10: LICENSING

They arrived with paper first.

The first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.

A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings.

Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe.

And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight.

“Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat.

Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.”

Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.”

Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.”

He touched the badge once. Like it burned.

“I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.”

The grin came back, wrong-shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.”

Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?”

Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.”

“By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.”

“Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.”

The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.”

“Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said.

“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”

“That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.”

“It is the issuing body.”

“Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?”

“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”

Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?”

“Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.”

Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.”

Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.”

“At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said.

“Don’t start,” Jhace warned.

“I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.”

Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.”

The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.”

“For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.”

He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?”

Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.”

Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”

“Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.”

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.”

She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.”

The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—”

“Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.”

Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.”

“The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.”

“Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.”

Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.”

“‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.”

“Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said.

“So does not crushing them,” Jhace said.

“This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality.

“Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

Jhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still.

When it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices.

Tallec lingered in the doorway. “Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.”

Jhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”

Tallec laughed and walked away.

The grid-hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic.

The doubled harmonic kept humming under the floor like a threat that didn’t need words.

[ASSISTANT]
## CHAPTER 10: LICENSING

They arrived with paper first.

The first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.

A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like the workshop had become an altar.

Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple Observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe.

And at the center—

Bram Tallow.

Different coat than usual. Administrator’s cut. Badge pinned.

Still Bram. But now carrying Crown weight.

Jhace felt his tremor spike the moment he saw the badge.

“Don’t start,” Jhace said.

Bram’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.”

Jhace stared at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.”

Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.”

He touched the badge once. Like it burned.

“I’m terrified,” Bram said, quiet enough to almost count as honesty. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.”

The grin came back wrong-shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.”

Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it.”

Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.”

The clerk didn’t look up. “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.”

“Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.”

The clerk flattened the top sheet with two fingers. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement.”

He spoke like he was reading a tool inventory.

“You will not manifest outside authorized events.”

Tiffani’s gaze didn’t move off the form. “Define ‘deployment authority.’”

“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”

“That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.”

“It is the issuing body.”

“Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?”

“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”

Jhace tasted copper.

Not flood. Not activation.

Just the baseline tang that had taken up residence and refused to leave.

“What happens if we refuse,” Jhace asked.

The clerk’s pen paused. “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.”

Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.”

Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.”

“At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said.

“Don’t start,” Jhace warned.

“I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.”

Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him.

The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.”

“For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.”

His eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?”

Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.”

Jhace put his hands flat on the bench because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. The tremor ticked under his palms like a machine that wouldn’t shut off.

“Next time I hold,” Jhace said, “it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”

“Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.”

Tiffani didn’t look at Bram. Didn’t look at Tallec. Stayed on the form.

“If we don’t sign,” she said, “we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.”

She set her pen down with a small click. Final. “We sign. But our standard rides with it.”

The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—”

“Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.”

Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.”

“The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.”

“Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.”

Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.”

“‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.”

“Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said.

“So does not crushing them,” Jhace said.

“This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality.

“Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

Jhace stared at the line waiting for his name.

Blank space pretending it was consent.

He signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still.

Ink wobble. Letters jittered. His name looked like it was trying to crawl off the paper.

The clerk rolled the forms back up when it was done and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices.

Tallec lingered in the doorway.

“Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.”

Jhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”

Tallec laughed and walked away.

The grid-hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic.

It didn’t care about seals.

It didn’t care about badges.

It just kept humming like a threat that didn’t need words.

1:N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDSTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)---CHAPTER 1: THE BELLThe bell rang wrong.Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.He didn’t go back for it.His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.One. Two. Three. Four.The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.Copper.Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.He didn't.Underface rule: waste nothing.His hands started shaking.Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.And then the tremor came back twice as fast.It wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.The tremor kept going.He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.Still shaking.It wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."Jhace looked at the structure.The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.The people inside didn't have minutes."I'm not—" Jhace started.The heat arrived.Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.His chest burned hotter.The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.He didn't know what he was doing.He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.He put both hands on the main support column.Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.The building stopped moving.It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count."Move," he rasped. "Get them out."Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.She didn't look at him like a miracle.She looked at him like a system that could fail."How long can you hold?" she demanded.Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know.""Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away."I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth."Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.One hundred.One hundred twenty.His arms started to tremble.Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else."Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.He didn't answer. Answering took breath.At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash."How many?" he rasped.The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.Jhace's hands shook harder."No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb."Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission."Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.Jhace let the filament go.The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.He staggered back into open air.His hands wouldn't stop shaking.The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name.""Jhace Torrins."She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two-forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway."Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain."If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.It didn’t. Not fully.A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.He nodded once."Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."The woman's slate scratched.She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.Behind them, the bell rang again.Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.---CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERNThe theodolite wouldn't settle.Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.The crosshair still drifted.No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.Vibration.Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:Tide 0 — Kindling  Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.Her mouth tasted copper.She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went[... ELLIPSIZATION ...] DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.Chloen read the first line.YOU CAN SAY NO.She looked up. “That’s new.”“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.The door clicked. The room held its breath.Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”He left.Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.Tiffani didn’t move.She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.---CODA: GLASS IN THE HANDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.Jhace tried to sleep.Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.---CODA: THE SHARDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.Then the breathing stopped.No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.The door opened anyway.Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.A figure stepped in.No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.They didn't speak.They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.Jhace tried to jerk away.Nothing.He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.Nothing.The figure took that hand anyway.Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.Something cold settled into the center of his palm.A shard.Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.Light threaded up from his palm.Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.But he felt the line.He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.Then they stepped back.No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.The door shut without sound.The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.The tremor was gone.In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.---END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS---

[ASSISTANT]
import re, pathlib, textwrap
text = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data/N01Final.Last.txt").read_text(errors="ignore")
# normalize newlines
t = text.replace('\r\n','\n').replace('\r','\n')
# find chapter headers
headers = [(m.group(0), m.start()) for m in re.finditer(r"CHAPTER\s+\d+:\s+[A-Z0-9 \-\(\)']+", t)]

[TOOL]
1:N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDSTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)---CHAPTER 1: THE BELLThe bell rang wrong.Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.He didn’t go back for it.His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.One. Two. Three. Four.The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.Copper.Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.He didn't.Underface rule: waste nothing.His hands started shaking.Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.And then the tremor came back twice as fast.It wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.The tremor kept going.He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.Still shaking.It wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."Jhace looked at the structure.The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.The people inside didn't have minutes."I'm not—" Jhace started.The heat arrived.Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.His chest burned hotter.The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.He didn't know what he was doing.He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.He put both hands on the main support column.Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.The building stopped moving.It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count."Move," he rasped. "Get them out."Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.She didn't look at him like a miracle.She looked at him like a system that could fail."How long can you hold?" she demanded.Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know.""Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away."I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth."Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.One hundred.One hundred twenty.His arms started to tremble.Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else."Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.He didn't answer. Answering took breath.At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash."How many?" he rasped.The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.Jhace's hands shook harder."No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb."Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission."Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.Jhace let the filament go.The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.He staggered back into open air.His hands wouldn't stop shaking.The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name.""Jhace Torrins."She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two-forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway."Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain."If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.It didn’t. Not fully.A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.He nodded once."Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."The woman's slate scratched.She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.Behind them, the bell rang again.Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.---CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERNThe theodolite wouldn't settle.Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.The crosshair still drifted.No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.Vibration.Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:Tide 0 — Kindling  Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.Her mouth tasted copper.She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This[... ELLIPSIZATION ...] DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.Chloen read the first line.YOU CAN SAY NO.She looked up. “That’s new.”“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.The door clicked. The room held its breath.Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”He left.Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.Tiffani didn’t move.She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.---CODA: GLASS IN THE HANDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.Jhace tried to sleep.Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.---CODA: THE SHARDJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.Then the breathing stopped.No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.The door opened anyway.Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.A figure stepped in.No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.They didn't speak.They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.Jhace tried to jerk away.Nothing.He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.Nothing.The figure took that hand anyway.Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.Something cold settled into the center of his palm.A shard.Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.Light threaded up from his palm.Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.But he felt the line.He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.Then they stepped back.No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.The door shut without sound.The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.The tremor was gone.In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.---END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS---

[TOOL]
CHAPTER 11: HUNGER
The hunger wasn’t metaphor.
It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer.
Tiffani called it activation pressure.
Jhace called it wanting.
He sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep.
Daved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.
“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.
Jhace frowned. “What does?”
“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”
Jhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed.
“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”
Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”
Jhace frowned. “What does.”
“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”
Daved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”
Jhace’s head snapped. “What.”
“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”
“I don’t want to chase the rumor.”
“You want it anyway.”
Jhace didn’t answer. His tremor did.
“That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said.
“He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.”
Jhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.”
“Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said.
“Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.”
“Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said.
“No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.”
Daved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.”
“Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.”
Jhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”
“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.
“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”
“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.

Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training.
Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid.
He hated himself for that flare.
He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.
Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors.
Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:
“Hungry hands hold until they break.”
Jhace pretended he didn’t hear.
He did.
---

[ASSISTANT]
## CHAPTER 11: HUNGER — VARIANT A

The hunger wasn’t metaphor.

It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid-hum shifted, whenever a bell tried to ring, whenever someone screamed the word *hold* like it was prayer.

Tiffani called it activation pressure.

Jhace called it wanting.

He sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep. The room still smelled like antiseptic resin and copper you couldn’t wash off. The cot frame creaked with every small movement, like even rest was load.

Daved’s eyes opened.

He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.

“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.

Jhace frowned. “What does?”

“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”

Jhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed without permission.

“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”

Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”

Jhace’s frown deepened. “What does.”

“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat. Perceived control. Reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”

Daved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”

Jhace’s head snapped. “What.”

“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”

Jhace’s jaw worked. His hands stayed jammed under his thighs like that could pin the tremor down. “I don’t want to chase the rumor.”

“You want it anyway.”

Tiffani’s pen didn’t pause. “Denial expected. The body remembers the moment it stopped failing.”

Jhace swallowed. Copper sat on his tongue like a coin he couldn’t spit out.

“Don’t make it holy,” Daved rasped.

“No one’s making it holy,” Bram said from the doorway, because Bram couldn’t let silence stand there unchallenged. “They’re making it *useful.* Much worse.”

Tiffani didn’t look up. “We’re not discussing theology. We’re discussing compulsion.”

Jhace stared at Daved’s hands. The tremor there was smaller than it had been on Tide one, but it existed. Proof the body came back wrong, even when it came back.

Daved watched Jhace watch.

“You think method will save you,” Daved said.

“It will keep us alive,” Tiffani said.

Daved’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “Alive enough to do it again.”

Jhace felt the warmth behind his sternum shift at the thought. Not flare. Just a quiet readiness.

He hated that readiness.

Tiffani finally lifted her eyes. “If you feel the pressure rising, you report it. Out loud. You don’t sit there and pretend you’re made of iron.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “I’m not pretending.”

“Yes you are,” Daved said. “You’ve been pretending since Junction Seven.”

Jhace went still for three heartbeats. The tremor didn’t.

Tiffani’s voice stayed flat. “We decide when we deploy. Not the heat. Not the crowd. Not the Crown.”

Jhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”

“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.

“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”

“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.

Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse.

A drill signal.

Crown-mandated training.

Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway. Eager. Stupid.

He hated himself for that flare.

He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.

Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many stands on stone floors.

Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:

“Hungry hands hold until they break.”

Jhace pretended he didn’t hear.

He did.

This is different from Chapter 10. In Chapter 10, ChatGPT enhanced while maintaining all canon. Here, ChatGPT added a character (Bram) not present in base manuscript.
Options:
A) Lock GROK - Pure base manuscript match, no character additions
B) Lock CHATGPT - Enhanced texture BUT includes non-canon Bram cameo
C) Request ChatGPT revision - Remove Bram, keep other enhancements
My recommendation: Option C - The sensory details and Junction Seven callback are excellent additions that match your established style, but adding Bram where he doesn't exist in canon is a violation we should correct.

this is the grok version
## CHAPTER 11: HUNGER

The hunger wasn’t metaphor.

It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid-hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word *hold* like it was prayer.

Tiffani called it activation pressure.

Jhace called it wanting.

He sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep.

Daved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.

“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.

Jhace frowned. “What does?”

“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”

Jhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed.

“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”

Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”

Jhace frowned. “What does.”

“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”

Daved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”

Jhace’s head snapped. “What.”

“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”

“I don’t want to chase the rumor.”

“You want it anyway.”

Jhace didn’t answer. His tremor did.

“That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said.

“He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.”

Jhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.”

“Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said.

“Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.”

“Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said.

“No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.”

Daved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.”

“Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.”

Jhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”

“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.

“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”

“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.

Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown-mandated training.

Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid.

He hated himself for that flare.

He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.

Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors.

Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:

“Hungry hands hold until they break.”

Jhace pretended he didn’t hear.

He did.

Lock **Grok Chapter 11**.

Reason: it stays inside base scope and doesn’t introduce Bram. It also cleanly bridges into Crown drill/training without dragging extra institutional chatter in-scene.

Proceeding to **Chapter 12: TRAINING YARD — Variant A**:

## CHAPTER 12: TRAINING YARD — VARIANT A

The yard was not a yard.

It was a fenced square of stone behind Compliance, scraped clean of debris and made to look safe by removing anything that could tell the truth.

Lines were chalked on the ground like geometry could replace judgment.

A bell hung on a freestanding iron frame.

Not a street bell.

A training bell.

It rang once.

Sharp. Controlled. Insulting.

Jhace stood with four other marked operators in a loose line, hands shaking in the open air. No pockets to hide them in. No bench to press them flat against.

Just tremor.

Just the doubled hum under the stones like a low animal.

Tiffani stood off to the side with her slate. Not part of the line. Not invited. Not welcome.

She wrote anyway.

A Crown instructor paced in front of them, boots too clean for the city they claimed to serve. No dust on his cuffs. No copper-gold grit caught in his seams.

He pointed at the bell frame. “You will manifest on signal.”

Jhace tasted copper.

Baseline. Permanent.

The instructor kept pacing. “You will terminate on signal.”

Jhace’s chest warmed.

Not much.

Just enough to be a problem.

A clerk stood behind the instructor with a slate and a ledger stamp. He watched like this was inventory audit.

Jhace’s mouth tightened.

Tiffani’s pen scratched once: **Oversight present. Registry enforcement in practice.**

The instructor stopped in front of Jhace. Looked at his hands.

“Name,” he said.

“Jhace Torrins.”

“Licensed,” the instructor said, like the word changed the tremor. “Capability: structural stabilization. Cost markers: tremor, visual artifacts, epistaxis.”

Jhace didn’t correct him.

He didn’t want to hear his own breakdown recited like tool wear.

The instructor turned to the line. “When the bell rings, you place palms on the brace. You hold. You release on my bell. No deviations.”

A guard shifted at the fence line. Not subtle. Not pretending he wasn’t there to enforce.

Tiffani’s voice cut in. “Your bell cadence is not the street cadence.”

The instructor didn’t look at her. “This is controlled.”

“Controlled doesn’t mean comparable,” Tiffani said. “Your onset is artificial. Your stress pattern is artificial. You will produce false confidence.”

The instructor’s jaw tightened. He finally looked at her. “Are you licensed?”

“No.”

“Then you are not authorized to instruct.”

Tiffani didn’t blink. “Then you are not authorized to be wrong.”

A ripple went through the operator line. Not laughter. Recognition. Relief. Someone saying what their hands couldn’t.

The clerk’s stamp hovered.

The instructor’s mouth went flat. “Silence.”

Jhace felt the warmth behind his sternum answer the command like it liked being ordered.

He hated that.

The bell rang again.

One strike.

Signal.

The brace in front of them was a thick iron column bolted into the stone. A fake load. A fake failure. But the hum in the metal was real enough to make Jhace’s molars ache.

Jhace stepped forward.

His tremor spiked.

He put both palms on the brace.

Heat flared behind his sternum—familiar, immediate—and copper-gold filament threaded from his hands into the iron seam.

The brace didn’t move.

It wasn’t going to.

That was the point.

But the light still came.

And his body still paid.

Jhace’s breath shook out of him.

He felt the reinforcement loop Daved had named without meaning to: chest heat, perceived control, the stupid relief of the city obeying for a heartbeat.

Wanting.

The instructor watched his hands. “Hold steady.”

Jhace’s tremor ran up his forearms like it wanted to climb into his shoulders again.

Tiffani wrote: **Onset immediate despite non-load. Reinforcement loop confirmed.**

The instructor raised his hand.

Jhace’s chest heat flared harder, eager, stupid, like it wanted the signal more than it wanted air.

The instructor rang the training bell—two quick strikes.

Terminate.

Jhace tried to let go.

For a heartbeat his hands didn’t listen.

Not full lock.

A hesitation.

A drag.

Then the filament snapped back into nothing and Jhace stumbled a half-step away from the brace like he’d been yanked loose.

His hands shook worse.

The clerk stamped something.

Tiffani’s pen scratched fast: **Termination latency observed. Micro-delay. Potential precursor.**

Jhace stared at his own fingers.

He hadn’t wanted to hold the brace.

His body had.

The instructor moved down the line to the next operator as if Jhace was just a successful test.

“Again,” the instructor said, to the group. “We repeat until compliance is consistent.”

Tiffani’s voice stayed low, but it carried. “If you repeat the loop without load, you will train addiction.”

The instructor didn’t turn. “We are training control.”

Jhace tasted copper.

His chest warmed again.

Eager and stupid.

He hated himself for it.

He stepped back into line, hands shaking in the open air, while the yard pretended it could teach something the street would later punish.
