## CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER — VARIANT A

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand.

Not because she wanted to prove something.

Because her right hand refused to stay still and her knee refused to lock without a fight.

It wasn’t Stage. Not the way she logged it for operators.

It was labor-fatigue. Joint-deep. The kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.

The ink pooled anyway.

Copper-gold dust clung to the nib and turned every line slightly wrong, like the city itself wanted the record to smear.

She made three piles.

Four, if she was honest.

One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Safety words. Protection words. Ownership words hidden in the seams.

One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of writing that looked safe until somebody had to bleed under it.

One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands you could carry in your head when paper got burned or stolen.

The last pile wasn’t for anyone.

Her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.

Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.

“You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up.

Bram glanced down at the badge. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.”

“You’re late.”

“Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.”

Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.”

Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?”

“This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the top page hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.”

Bram squinted. “And the part where we tell them to go fuck themselves?”

Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.”

Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.”

“It’s survivable,” she said. “Corlexi will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”

“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”

She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dark.

Operator refusal authority. Independent verification. No deployment by seal alone.

Bram stared at the words like they were a death sentence with a promotion. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse in a nicer coat.”

“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”

Bram laughed too loud for the room. “I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”

Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Underface next.”

Chloen arrived without knocking, like always.

Stone dust in her hair. Slate tucked under her arm. Eyes that didn’t waste motion on sentiment.

She didn’t look at Jhace’s cot.

She looked at the paper.

“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”

Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”

Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”

“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”

Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack.

Folded into a narrow strip. Tied with cord like a bandage.

Chloen read the first line.

**YOU CAN SAY NO.**

She looked up. “That’s new.”

“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”

Chloen flipped through.

Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. Termination cadence.

**If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.**

Then again:

**YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.**

Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”

Bram pointed at the strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”

Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”

Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.

The door clicked.

The room held its breath.

Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d waited for the Underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to exist.

“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”

Bram’s grin appeared instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”

Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started circulating language. Unlicensed manifestation as endangerment. It’s already being used.”

Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Case summaries. Enough to prove pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.

Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”

Tiffani’s pen didn’t pause. “Then it will be accurate.”

Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi.”

Tiffani finally looked up.

Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled. Not calm.

“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown that, they will call it law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”

Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. Adorable.”

“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”

Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”

“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”

Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”

He left.

Bram remained.

Shifted his weight like the coat itself was a burden.

“So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine,” he said.

“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”

Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”

Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is in a cot because his body decided he was a brace instead of a man. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”

She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.

“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, like even he could hear the edge.

Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard off the forge floor.

The paper took fast. Hungry. Like it had been waiting for permission.

Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”

“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.

Outside the infirmary room, an alert bell rang—thin, distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they’d been caught stealing.

Tiffani didn’t move.

She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.

---

### CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw.

The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin he couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls.

Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth.

His chest still rose. His heart still counted like it was a job.

Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers.

The tremor stalled for three heartbeats.

Perfect stillness.

It scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

---

### CODA: THE SHARD

Jhace lay flat in the cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

The guard outside kept breathing.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not corridor spill. It cut a clean rectangle across the floor and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

Male.

Clean cloth. No soot. No sweat. No perfume.

A faint copper-gold line sat under his shirt at the center of his chest—steady, embedded, like something had taken root behind his sternum and decided it belonged there.

His boots didn’t track dust.

His shadow didn’t arrive the way shadows should.

He didn’t speak.

He moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb over pupil. Then two fingers on his wrist—pulse point—patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The man took that hand anyway.

Both of his, around Jhace’s, and he unfolded Jhace’s fingers like breaking a joint open, careful and firm, no apology. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of Jhace’s palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first.

Deep cold, the kind that made teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament’s strain.

This heat arrived already aligned.

Like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow.

A thin copper-gold line that behaved like it had instructions, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam.

Elbow to shoulder.

Shoulder to sternum.

A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs.

Couldn’t feel the sheets.

Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for Tides settle into him—deep, precise—like the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The man closed Jhace’s fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then he stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if Jhace understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness.

Total.

Heavy.

Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone coughed. Someone laughed at something ordinary.

Ordinary noises.

While Jhace’s palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[USER]
## CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.

It wasn't Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.

The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.

She made three piles.

Four, if she was honest.

One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.

She wrote "deployment authority" first. Crossed it out. Rewrote: "deployment procedure."

Surface control. Let Corlexi read what he wanted to see.

One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.

One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.

The last pile wasn't for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.

Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he'd stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.

His badge caught lamplight. Clean metal. Official weight.

He touched it once like testing if it would burn.

"You look like a priest," Tiffani said without looking up.

Bram glanced down at the coat. "Don't you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I'm doing it recreationally."

"You're late."

"Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue." He leaned in, saw the piles. "Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment."

Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. "This goes to Corlexi."

Bram's fingers hovered over the top page. "This the 'please don't leash us' letter?"

"This is the 'you get what I give you' packet," she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. Ink on her thumb now. Copper-tinted. "Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no."

Bram squinted at the title line. "And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?"

Tiffani didn't blink. "Not included."

Bram's smile went sharp. "That's either genius or suicidal."

"It's survivable." She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. "He will read what he wants to read. I'm controlling the surface."

Bram exhaled through his nose. "Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor."

"There is no trapdoor," she said. "There are exits. Distributed."

She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: *Property is not a priority class.* *Operator veto.* *Refusal authority.* *No deployment without independent verification.*

Her knee throbbed where it refused to straighten. She shifted weight without comment.

Some costs weren't Stage markers. Some were just carrying the work.

Bram whistled low. "This one's the 'burn the Crown down' packet."

"This one is the 'keep operators alive long enough to matter' packet." She slid it closer. "Guild custody. You're the custodian."

Bram stared at the words *operator refusal authority* like they were a weapon and a confession. "They catch me with this, I'm a corpse with a promotion."

"They catch you without it, you're already dead," Tiffani said. "Just slower. With a better title."

Bram laughed, too loud for the room. "God, I hate when you're right. It's like being corrected by a scalpel."

Tiffani's pen scratched again. "Underface next."

Her right hand cramped. She switched the pen to her left without pausing.

Ambidextrous by fatigue. A skill she hadn't asked for.

Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek—purple-green, three Tides old at least. She didn't look at the bed. She didn't look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.

"Crown's sniffing," Chloen said. "Foremen too. They heard there's a registry. Means there's a list. Means there's a leash."

Bram lifted a hand. "Hi. I'm the leash's embarrassing middleman."

Chloen's mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn't trust. "You always this loud?"

"Only when I'm terrified," Bram said. "So yes."

Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.

Chloen read the first line.

**YOU CAN SAY NO.**

She looked up. "That's new."

"It's old," Tiffani said. "It just wasn't written down."

Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. "Wedges first." "Hold only what's needed." "Terminate on signal." "If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe."

And then, again: **YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.**

Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. "Good. Because schedules don't give a damn about ribs."

Bram pointed at the underface strip. "See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats."

Tiffani didn't look at him. "Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure."

Chloen nodded once and left like she'd been given a weapon and didn't want to be seen holding it.

The door clicked. The room held its breath.

Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he'd been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.

"For the record," he said, voice formal out of habit, "I'm not here."

Bram's grin came instantly. "For the record, I'm a virgin."

Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. "They've started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation."

Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.

She'd written it with both hands when the cramp got too bad. Her handwriting shifted mid-page. Legible. Uneven.

Evidence of labor. Not Stage.

Corran thumbed the top page. "This will get you killed."

Tiffani's pen didn't stop. "Then it will be accurate."

Corran's mouth tightened. "What did you withhold from Corlexi?"

Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.

"Consent," she said. "The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don't give it, they have to steal it in daylight."

Bram snorted. "She's making them commit crimes on paper. That's adorable."

"It's leverage," Tiffani said. "The kind that stains."

Corran tucked the packet into his binder. "Where does this go?"

"Somewhere it can't be conveniently lost," Tiffani said. "Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office."

Corran nodded once. "For the record, I didn't take that."

He left.

Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. The badge caught light again. He touched it like checking if it had gotten heavier.

"So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine," he said.

"He gets safety with ownership language," Tiffani corrected. "He will build a machine either way. I'm deciding where it jams."

Bram looked at the clean packet again. "You sure this doesn't just get people hurt?"

Tiffani's jaw tightened. Her knee locked finally—sharp, sudden—then released. She didn't log the timing. She didn't measure the interval.

Some failures weren't for the ledger.

"People are already hurt," she said. "Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest."

She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn't for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.

Cost markers she couldn't justify. Observations she wouldn't confess. The parts where method looked like murder and she'd chosen anyway.

"Not this one," Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.

Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal.

The paper caught fast. Hungry.

The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.

Smoke rose thin and copper-tinted. She watched it go.

Confession as carbon. Untraceable.

Bram watched the burn. "You just set fire to your own confession."

"I set fire to a target," Tiffani said.

Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.

Tiffani didn't move.

She dipped the pen again.

Her right hand this time. The cramp had passed.

She wrote the next copy anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
This Chapter 13 is solid and much tighter than the direction I drifted (especially by **not** stapling on extra “mystery payload” beats that belong elsewhere). It reads like it knows exactly what job it’s doing: **turn method into a weaponized document**, split distribution, and show Tiffani’s cost without pretending she’s an operator.

### What’s working (the important stuff)
- **Voice + compression**: clean, sharp, operational. Fragment spikes land where they should.
- **Tiffani cost without magic-creep**: knee refusal + hand cramp as labor, not Stage. That’s exactly the vibe you’ve been building: *the body pays whether it glows or not*.
- **The three/four piles concept**: great structural storytelling. You can feel the network forming.
- **Surface control vs. real control**: “deployment authority → deployment procedure” is a nasty little move. It’s believable and political without turning into a lecture.
- **Bram tone**: he’s used as pressure relief *without* diluting stakes. The “priest” line is perfect.

### What’s slightly off / worth tightening (small, fixable)
- **Repetition density**: a couple ideas repeat too close together (“badge caught lamplight / touched it once” happens multiple times). Pick one strong instance, don’t keep re-tapping it.
- **Over-explaining in a few spots**: lines like “Confession as carbon. Untraceable.” are cool, but they flirt with authorial commentary. One of those max; otherwise it starts smelling like you’re admiring the metaphor instead of just doing the scene.
- **Chloen bruise detail**: it’s good, but “three Tides old at least” is a tiny speed bump—either make it do something (ties to foreman violence / pressure) or cut it and keep her entrance razor-clean.
- **The “men in the hall stopped talking” line**: strong image, but “like they’d been caught stealing” is one of the only comparisons here that leans a bit cute. It’s not wrong, it’s just slightly “writerly” compared to the rest of the chapter’s bluntness.

### Canon/continuity check
- No forbidden real-world time units. Good.
- No new invented institutions or off-scope events. Good.
- Keeps the **post-Stage Four** reality in the room without rehashing it. Good.

### Recommendation
Lock this as **CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER**.

If you want it even meaner, the only “edit pass” I’d do later is a micro-trim pass: remove 3–6 repeated emphasis beats (badge touch/lamplight, trapdoor/exits, confession/carbon) and let the chapter stay knife-thin.

If you say “LOCK 13,” I’ll treat it as immutable canon and proceed to draft Chapter 14 Variant A.

[ASSISTANT]
## CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER — REVISED (REPEATS TRIMMED)

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.

It wasn't Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.

The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.

She made three piles.

Four, if she was honest.

One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.

She wrote "deployment authority" first. Crossed it out. Rewrote: "deployment procedure."

Surface control. Let Corlexi read what he wanted to see.

One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.

One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.

The last pile wasn't for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.

Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he'd stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.

His badge caught lamplight. Clean metal. Official weight.

"You look like a priest," Tiffani said without looking up.

Bram glanced down at the coat. "Don't you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I'm doing it recreationally."

"You're late."

"Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue." He leaned in, saw the piles. "Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment."

Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. "This goes to Corlexi."

Bram's fingers hovered over the top page. "This the 'please don't leash us' letter?"

"This is the 'you get what I give you' packet," she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. Ink on her thumb now. Copper-tinted. "Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no."

Bram squinted at the title line. "And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?"

Tiffani didn't blink. "Not included."

Bram's smile went sharp. "That's either genius or suicidal."

"It's survivable," she said. "He will read what he wants to read. I'm controlling the surface."

Bram exhaled through his nose. "Compliance theater."

"There are exits," she said. "Distributed."

She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: *Property is not a priority class.* *Operator veto.* *Refusal authority.* *No deployment without independent verification.*

Her knee throbbed where it refused to straighten. She shifted weight without comment.

Bram whistled low. "This one's the 'burn the Crown down' packet."

"This one is the 'keep operators alive long enough to matter' packet." She slid it closer. "Guild custody. You're the custodian."

Bram stared at the words *operator refusal authority* like they were a weapon and a confession. "They catch me with this, I'm a corpse with a promotion."

"They catch you without it, you're already dead," Tiffani said. "Just slower. With a better title."

Bram laughed, too loud for the room. "God, I hate when you're right. It's like being corrected by a scalpel."

Tiffani's pen scratched again. "Underface next."

Her right hand cramped. She switched the pen to her left without pausing.

Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. She didn't look at the bed. She didn't look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.

"Crown's sniffing," Chloen said. "Foremen too. They heard there's a registry. Means there's a list. Means there's a leash."

Bram lifted a hand. "Hi. I'm the leash's embarrassing middleman."

Chloen's mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn't trust. "You always this loud?"

"Only when I'm terrified," Bram said. "So yes."

Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.

Chloen read the first line.

**YOU CAN SAY NO.**

She looked up. "That's new."

"It's old," Tiffani said. "It just wasn't written down."

Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. "Wedges first." "Hold only what's needed." "Terminate on signal." "If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe."

And then, again: **YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.**

Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. "Good. Because schedules don't give a damn about ribs."

Bram pointed at the underface strip. "See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats."

Tiffani didn't look at him. "Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure."

Chloen nodded once and left like she'd been given a weapon and didn't want to be seen holding it.

The door clicked. The room held its breath.

Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he'd been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.

"For the record," he said, voice formal out of habit, "I'm not here."

Bram's grin came instantly. "For the record, I'm a virgin."

Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. "They've started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation."

Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.

Her handwriting shifted mid-page. Legible. Uneven.

Corran thumbed the top page. "This will get you killed."

Tiffani's pen didn't stop. "Then it will be accurate."

Corran's mouth tightened. "What did you withhold from Corlexi?"

Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.

"Consent," she said. "The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don't give it, they have to steal it in daylight."

Bram snorted. "She's making them commit crimes on paper. That's adorable."

"It's leverage," Tiffani said. "The kind that stains."

Corran tucked the packet into his binder. "Where does this go?"

"Somewhere it can't be conveniently lost," Tiffani said. "Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office."

Corran nodded once. "For the record, I didn't take that."

He left.

Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden.

"So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine," he said.

"He gets safety with ownership language," Tiffani corrected. "He will build a machine either way. I'm deciding where it jams."

Bram looked at the clean packet again. "You sure this doesn't just get people hurt?"

Tiffani's jaw tightened. Her knee locked finally—sharp, sudden—then released. She didn't log the timing.

"People are already hurt," she said. "Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest."

She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn't for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.

Bram’s voice dropped. "Not this one."

Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal.

The paper caught fast. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.

Bram watched the burn. "You just set fire to your own confession."

"I set fire to a target," Tiffani said.

Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking.

Tiffani didn't move.

She dipped the pen again.

Her right hand this time. The cramp had passed.

She wrote the next copy anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
Locked.

### CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw.

The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin he couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls.

Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth.

His chest still rose. His heart still counted like it was a job.

Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers.

The tremor stalled for three heartbeats.

Perfect stillness.

It scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

---

### CODA: THE SHARD

Jhace lay flat in the cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

The guard outside kept breathing.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not corridor spill. It cut a clean rectangle across the floor and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

Male.

Clean cloth. No soot. No sweat. No perfume.

A faint copper-gold line sat under his shirt at the center of his chest—steady, embedded, like something had taken root behind his sternum and decided it belonged there.

His boots didn’t track dust.

His shadow didn’t arrive the way shadows should.

He didn’t speak.

He moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb over pupil. Then two fingers on his wrist—pulse point—patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The man took that hand anyway.

Both of his, around Jhace’s, and he unfolded Jhace’s fingers like breaking a joint open, careful and firm, no apology. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of Jhace’s palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first.

Deep cold, the kind that made teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not filament strain.

This heat arrived already aligned.

Like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow.

A thin copper-gold line that behaved like it had instructions, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam.

Elbow to shoulder.

Shoulder to sternum.

A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs.

Couldn’t feel the sheets.

Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for Tides settle into him—deep, precise—like the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The man closed Jhace’s fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then he stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if Jhace understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness.

Total.

Heavy.

Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone coughed. Someone laughed at something ordinary.

Ordinary noises.

While Jhace’s palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[USER]
this was groks version
## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.

A guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

## CODA: THE SHARD

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.

A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.

They didn’t speak.

They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The figure took that hand anyway.

Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of his palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then they stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*

[ASSISTANT]
Grok’s codas are stronger than the ones I spit out. Cleaner sensory, better “infirmary object inventory,” and the Shard scene has that cold procedural cruelty you want.

But there’s one **hard violation** in Grok’s **CODA: THE SHARD**:

- **“for days”** → forbidden. Real-world time unit. Must be **Tides**.

Also a couple micro-repeats you can trim without losing punch (double “cot” opener; double “slate he couldn’t reach” across both codas is fine, but in CODA: THE SHARD it’s stacked a bit heavy).

Below is a **tight, compliant patch** of Grok’s codas. Same voice. Same moves. No “days.” No extra invention.

---

## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND (PATCHED)

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.

A guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

---

## CODA: THE SHARD (PATCHED)

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted like it was a job.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.

They didn’t speak.

They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The figure took that hand anyway.

Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of his palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for **Tides** arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then they stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*

---

If you want, I can also do a *meaner* trim pass (another ~3–5% shorter) without losing any information—just shaving duplicate inventory beats.

[USER]
## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. A tin cup on the side table, out of range. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.

A guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

## CODA: THE SHARD

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.

A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.

They didn’t speak.

They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The figure took that hand anyway.

Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of his palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since the Piercing arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then they stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*

[ASSISTANT]
Two fixes.

1) **“since the Piercing”** is fine. But you also used **“counted time”**. “Time” is borderline in-world, but it’s not a banned unit. If you want it cleaner: change to **“counted heartbeats”** (already your in-text metric).

2) Add one Te’Oga-unit anchor if you want the “for Tides” cadence without inventing duration. Right now it’s okay as-is.

Here’s the **minimal patch** (only the needed line edits):

### CODA: THE SHARD — micro-patch
- Replace: **“His heart still counted time like it was a job.”**  
  With: **“His heart still counted heartbeats like it was a job.”**

- Replace: **“He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since the Piercing arrive inside him instead—”**  
  With: **“He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since Kindling arrive inside him instead—”**

Everything else in your pasted version is clean and lockable.

## CODA: THE SHARD

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted heartbeats like it was a job.

A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The figure took that hand anyway.

Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of his palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since Kindling arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then they stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[USER]
now really, should there be two codas? shouldnt i make it one seemless coda?

[ASSISTANT]
One seamless coda is better.

Two codas reads like a DVD menu: “Bonus Scene 1 / Bonus Scene 2.” It also double-resets the same condition (“Jhace lay flat…”) and risks diffusing the punch. A coda should feel like a single closing mechanism: quiet → intrusion → irreversible change.

Keep the **GLASS IN THE HAND** inventory as the *front half* (your “aftertaste + tremor/stillness dread”), then let it slide into **THE SHARD** without a header break. Use one hard transition beat: the guard’s breathing stops.

If you still want the *idea* of two movements, do it with a midline divider or a single sentence paragraph, not a second titled section.

Here’s how it reads as **one seamless coda**, with minimal changes (mostly removing the second header and smoothing the join):

---

## CODA

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.

He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. A tin cup on the side table, out of range. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.

A guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.

Jhace tried to sleep.

Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.

He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.

Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.

Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.

“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.

Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.

Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted heartbeats like it was a job.

A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.

Then the breathing stopped.

No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.

The door opened anyway.

Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.

A figure stepped in.

No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.

They didn’t speak.

They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.

A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.

Jhace tried to jerk away.

Nothing.

He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.

Nothing.

The figure took that hand anyway.

Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.

Something cold settled into the center of his palm.

A shard.

Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.

Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.

Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.

Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.

Light threaded up from his palm.

Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.

It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.

Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.

But he felt the line.

He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since Kindling arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.

His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.

The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.

Then they stepped back.

No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.

The door shut without sound.

The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.

Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.

The tremor was gone.

In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.

Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*

import re
# find positions of chapter headers
chapters=[]
for m in re.finditer(r'\nCHAPTER\s+(\d+):\s+([^\n]+)\n', txt):
    chapters.append((int(m.group(1)), m.group(2).strip(), m.start()))
chapters[:5], chapters[-3:], len(chapters)

[TOOL]
([(1, 'THE BELL', 102),
  (2, 'THE PATTERN', 9011),
  (3, 'SECOND HOLD', 13276),
  (4, 'PROTOCOL', 21554),
  (5, 'FIRST NETWORK', 26369)],
 [(11, 'HUNGER', 56725), (12, 'METHOD', 59356), (13, 'THE LEDGER', 65103)],
 13)

[ASSISTANT]
# extract chapter 12 content from base
def extract_chapter(n):
    # find header
    pat = re.compile(r'\nCHAPTER\s+%d:\s+[^\n]+\n' % n)
    m=pat.search(txt)
    if not m: 
        return None
    start=m.end()
    # end at next chapter header or end
    m2=re.search(r'\nCHAPTER\s+%d:\s' % (n+1), txt)
    if m2:
        end=m2.start()
    else:
        end=len(txt)
    return txt[start:end].strip()

ch12=extract_chapter(12)
ch12[:500], ch12[-300:], len(ch12.splitlines())

Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\nShe pointed left, then f',
 '. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\n“Something like that.”\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”',
 94)

[ASSISTANT]
import re
forbidden = ["day","week","month","year","hour","minute","second"]
def find_forbidden(text):
    hits=[]
    for w in forbidden:
        for m in re.finditer(r'\b'+w+r's?\b', text, flags=re.IGNORECASE):
            hits.append((w, m.start(), text[m.start()-30:m.start()+30]))
    return hits
hits12=find_forbidden(ch12)
hits12[:10], len(hits12)

   'o a blade.\n“One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift')],
 2)

ch12_patched = ch12_patched.replace("hours without sleep", "Tides without sleep")
# verify no forbidden

[ASSISTANT]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  
## Chapters 1–13 + CODA — COMPILED MANUSCRIPT

---

## CHAPTER 1: THE BELL

The bell rang wrong.

Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant check seals, tighten brackets. This was the frantic peal of something already falling.

Jhace moved before the bell finished its third strike.

Grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore. Dumped them into his palm and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.

Didn't go back for it.

His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it.

One. Two. Three. Four.

The doubled hum hit him at Junction Twelve.

Skipped the pipe entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.

Copper.

Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.

He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.

Didn't.

Underface rule: waste nothing.

His hands started shaking.

Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.

And then the tremor came back twice as fast.

It wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.

He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.

Forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.

The tremor kept going.

Pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.

Still shaking.

It wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.

He'd seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He'd called it age.

Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.

Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.

Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.

A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."

Jhace looked at the structure.

The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.

No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.

The people inside didn't have Tides.

"I'm not—" Jhace started.

The heat arrived.

Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp.

Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.

He watched it climb his legs. Static-cling. Looking for him.

His chest burned hotter.

The doubled hum snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.

He didn't know what he was doing.

He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.

He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.

Put both hands on the main support column.

Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.

The building stopped moving.

It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.

Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment.

"Move," he rasped. "Get them out."

Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.

A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.

She didn't look at him with awe.

She looked at him with assessment.

"How long can you hold?" she demanded.

Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know."

"Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."

Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.

"I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth.

"Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."

People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.

His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.

One hundred.

One hundred twenty.

His arms started to tremble.

Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.

"Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.

He didn't answer. Answering took breath.

At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it.

"How many?" he rasped.

The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"

A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.

Jhace's hands shook harder.

"No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.

"Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."

A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried.

The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."

Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it.

"Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.

Jhace let the filament go.

The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.

He staggered back into open air.

His hands wouldn't stop shaking.

The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless.

The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name."

"Jhace Torrins."

She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two-forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.

"Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.

She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.

"If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."

Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.

Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.

It didn't. Not fully.

A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.

Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.

His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.

He pulled it away. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased.

That was the first time the hunger felt real.

Jhace's hands shook between them.

He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.

He nodded once.

"Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."

The woman's slate scratched.

She wrote: **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.**

Behind them, the bell rang again.

Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.

Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.

Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.

---

## CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN

The theodolite wouldn't settle.

Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.

The crosshair still drifted.

No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame.

She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.

Vibration.

Faint. Constant. The doubled hum lived under the normal grid pulse. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.

She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:

**Tide 0 — Kindling**  
**Lens frame vibration: doubled hum present.**  
**Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees.**  
**Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.**

Her mouth tasted copper.

She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue.

Swallowing felt like evidence.

The light outside the lens port changed.

The light didn't dim or brighten. It bent.

Tiffani looked up.

For a second she couldn't move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: *What the fuck is that?*

The sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.

A line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.

A spindle.

Long and narrow and wrong.

Her breath stopped.

For a stupid second, she couldn't make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised.

The spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.

Then gravity lurched.

Not a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.

Tiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.

The spindle was gone.

Punched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.

Pressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More resistance than heat. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.

Then copper-gold particulate began falling outside.

Glittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.

Tiffani touched it with her fingertip.

It didn't smear. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.

She wrote:

**Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.**  
**Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.**  
**Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**

Her pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.

A bell rang.

Not the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.

Then another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.

Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.

Tiffani's stomach tightened.

She grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.

The doubled hum followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.

At the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.

Tallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.

A third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.

Closer—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed.

Tiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.

She ran toward Sector Nine.

The copper taste got stronger with every stride.

---

## CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD

The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.

The collapse wasn't honest.

Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.

Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.

He'd been here five breath-counts.

His boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting.

Too close. Too human.

The tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.

The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.

A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.

"You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again."

Jhace pulled free. "I don't—"

"There's forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got."

The copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.

His chest heat flared.

The doubled hum he'd felt in the grid arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned.

He didn't want this.

He didn't understand this.

He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.

"Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting."

He stepped under the sagging upper floor.

The heat detonated behind his sternum.

Put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker.

The warehouse stopped settling.

The groan of stressed iron went quiet.

Jhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.

The tremor in his arms worsened.

Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.

"Move!" someone shouted. "Get them out!"

Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.

A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.

She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.

"You're the Tallow Street hold," she said.

Not a question. A confirmation.

"Yeah," Jhace managed.

"How long have you been holding?"

"I don't know. Five breath-counts? Ten?"

Her pen scratched. "Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible."

"What?" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.

"Your body is paying for this," she said, still writing. "If it locks past tolerance, you won't be able to let go."

"I can't let go," Jhace said through clenched teeth. "Not until they're out."

The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. "Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside."

"Then I hold for forty."

"Then you might not walk away."

Jhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. "I already know that."

The woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate.
