The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they’d been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.



CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can’t spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.



CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive. They didn’t speak. They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.



END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS



CHAPTER 10: LICENSING

They arrived with paper first. The first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.

A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you’re done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

Jhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still. When it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices. Tallec lingered in the doorway. “Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.” Jhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” Tallec laughed and walked away. The grid‑hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic. The doubled harmonic kept humming under the floor like a threat that didn’t need words.



CHAPTER 11: HUNGER

The hunger wasn’t metaphor. It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer. Tiffani called it activation pressure. Jhace called it wanting. He sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep. Daved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed. “Feels… good,” Daved rasped. Jhace frowned. “What does?” “The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.” Jhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed. “And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.” Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.” Jhace frowned. “What does.” “The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.” Daved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.” Jhace’s head snapped. “What.” “You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.” “I don’t want to chase the rumor.” “You want it anyway.” Jhace didn’t answer. His tremor did. “That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said. “He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.” Jhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.” “Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said. “Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.” “Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said. “No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.” Daved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.” “Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.” Jhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.” “And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked. “We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.” “Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.

Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did.



CHAPTER 12: METHOD

The alert came at Tide 21 like an insult. It was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people. A residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse. “Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.” She pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.” The apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.

Jhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd. He could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower. Bram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact. “Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.” “Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.” “Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.” She looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade. “One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.” “Then who?” Jhace said. “Not you.” “Then who.” “The child is choking, Jhace.” Jhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.” Bram’s breath punched out. “Jesus—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.” Jhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood. For a heartbeat, nothing happened. Then the hold locked. The stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch. Tiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.” Boots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained. “Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!” Hands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life. “Terminate,” Tiffani said. Jhace didn’t move. “Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology. Silence. Bram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.” “Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.” Bram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.” “Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.” Jhace could still see. Still hear. Still count. He stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. There was no tremor. There was nothing. He tried to will a finger. Nothing. Panic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding. He tried to shout. Nothing left his throat. He tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing. He tried to blink faster—signal—anything. His lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered. The body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone. The coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready. Ready to hold. But he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no. Only the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.

Tiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep. “Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.” He blinked once. “Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?” Once. “Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?” Once. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split. Tiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened. “Can you predict where it fails next?” Once. Not a comfort. A capability. Tiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment. Jhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing. It was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.

But the numbers still lined up. The city still had a baseline. He could still calibrate it. Tiffani’s eyes tracked his fingers. “His hands are still.” Bram swallowed. “That’s… new.” “There is no tremor,” Tiffani said. “Great,” Bram said. “We fixed the tremor by removing the man. Efficient.” “Bram.” “Sorry,” Bram said. “I’m coping. Poorly. Loudly.” Tiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving. “Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.” “You’re writing this down,” Bram said. “Yes.” “Right now.” “Yes.” “While he’s—” “Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.” She swallowed once, hard, then kept writing. When the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood. “Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said. Bram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?” “A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.” “So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said. “Something like that.” “You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said. “I’m not calm,” Tiffani said. “Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”

CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they’d been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.



CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can’t spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.



CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive. They didn’t speak. They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.



END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS



CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they’d been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.



CODA

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can’t spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive. They didn’t speak. They moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

[TOOL]
“Go,” the woman shouted toward the breach. “Last sweep. Now.” A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for hours. The woman’s voice cut clean through the chaos. “Twelve out. Corridor clear.” Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission. “Terminate,” she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol. Jhace let the filament go. The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty. He staggered back into open air. His hands wouldn’t stop shaking. The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he’d prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost. The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. “Name.” “Jhace Torrins.” She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. “Duration: two‑forty—” The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway. “Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast. She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain. “If we don’t measure it,” she said, “we kill you next time.” Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates. Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade. It didn’t. Not fully. A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs. Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice. Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held. His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching. He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break. That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote. Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn’t wanted. He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing. He nodded once. “Measure,” he said. “Then make sure there isn’t a next time.” The woman’s slate scratched. She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0. Behind them, the bell rang again. Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat. Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air. Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.
6 
7 CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN
8 The theodolite wouldn’t settle. Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work. The crosshair still drifted. No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn’t looking. She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame. Vibration. Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble. She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate: Tide 0 — Kindling
Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.
Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.
Neither atmosphere nor mechanism. Her mouth tasted copper. She’d been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue like she’d been chewing wire. The light outside the lens port changed. The light didn’t dim or brighten. It bent. Tiffani looked up. For a second she couldn’t move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: What the fuck is that? The sky over Saint Merro wasn’t supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else. A line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing. A spindle. Long and narrow and wrong, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world and walked away. Her breath stopped. For a stupid second, she couldn’t make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised, as if she could physically stop the sky from doing whatever it was doing.
9 The spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin. Then gravity lurched. Not a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger, like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent. Tiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port. The spindle was gone. Punched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn’t tell. Pressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More like the city inhaling and forgetting how to exhale. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift. Then copper-gold particulate began falling outside. Glittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved. Tiffani touched it with her fingertip. It didn’t smear like ash. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin. She wrote: Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.
Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.
Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive? Her pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.
10 A bell rang. Not the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling. Then another bell. Different direction. Same cadence. Emergency. Emergency. Emergency. Tiffani’s stomach tightened. She grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran. The doubled harmonic followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn’t been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new. At the mid-terrace landing, she stopped. Tallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn’t parse. A third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district. Closer—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed. Tiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source. She ran toward Sector Nine. The copper taste got stronger with every stride.
11 
12 CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD
13 The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted. The collapse wasn’t honest. Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn’t match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves. Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still. He’d been here five minutes. His boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting. Too close. Too human.
14 The tremor from Tallow Street hadn’t stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle. The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting. A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise. “You held Tallow,” the foreman said. “Do it again.” Jhace pulled free. “I don’t—” “There’s forty people in there,” the foreman cut in. “Maybe more. Wedges won’t hold. Straps won’t reach. You’re what we’ve got.” The copper taste flooded Jhace’s mouth again. His chest heat flared. The doubled harmonic he’d felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building’s stress pattern aligned like they were the same system. He didn’t want this. He didn’t understand this. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn’t ignore. “Clear the approach,” Jhace said, voice rough. “Get spotters. I need someone counting.” He stepped under the sagging upper floor. The heat detonated behind his sternum. He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it. The warehouse stopped settling. The groan of stressed iron went quiet. Jhace’s breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response. The tremor in his arms worsened. Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen. “Move!” someone shouted. “Get them out!” Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble. A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth. She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders. “You’re the Tallow Street hold,” she said. Not a question. A confirmation. “Yeah,” Jhace managed. “How long have you been holding?” “I don’t know. Five minutes? Ten?” Her pen scratched. “Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible.” “What?” Jhace’s vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it. “Your body is paying for this,” she said, still writing even as her thumb cramped. “If it locks past tolerance, you won’t be able to let go.” “I can’t let go,” Jhace said through clenched teeth. “Not until they’re out.” The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. “Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside.” “Then I hold for forty.” “Then you might not walk away.” Jhace’s hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. “I already know that.” The woman’s jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror. “I’m documenting this,” she said. “Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don’t measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why.” “What’s your name?” Jhace asked. “Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory.” “Jhace Torrins.” “I know,” Tiffani said. “I wrote it down at Tallow Street.” She showed him her slate: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive. Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density. Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data. “What caused this?” he asked. Tiffani’s pen paused. “Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—” She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight. “—everywhere.” Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him. “The doubled harmonic,” he said. “You felt it too?” Tiffani’s eyes sharpened. “In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.” Tiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation. A rescue crew leader shouted. “Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.” Jhace’s vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked. “How long have I been holding?” he rasped. Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. “Eighteen minutes.” “Tallow Street was four.” “Your cost is accelerating,” Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. “Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable.” Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip. “Terminate after extraction,” Tiffani said. “Not before. But the instant they’re clear, you let go.” “What if I can’t?” “Then I make you.” The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely. “Clear!” the crew leader yelled. Tiffani’s hand closed on Jhace’s shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real. “Terminate,” she said. “Now.” Jhace let the filament go. The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty. Jhace staggered back. His legs didn’t hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone. Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving. Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination). Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded. Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. “You write everything like it’s going to behave.” “Everything does behave,” Tiffani said. “Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.” “It’s killing me in increments.” “Yes,” Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. “So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray.” Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data. “Why are you doing this?” he asked. Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain. “Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,” she said. “Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don’t measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.” A bell rang in the distance. Fourth collapse. Different sector. The copper taste surged in Jhace’s mouth. The heat in his chest flared. Tiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating. She looked at Jhace. “Can you stand?” “I don’t know.” “Try.” Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn’t stop trembling. But he stood. “Good,” Tiffani said. “Because we’re going to the next one. And I’m counting every heartbeat.” She walked toward the sound of the bell. Jhace followed. Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat. The bell kept ringing.
15 
16 CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
17 Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it. Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write. Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log. Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket. His hands shook. The grid‑hum didn’t break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth. He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone. He stared at it like it had betrayed him. Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.” “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence. “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.” Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care. He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable. “What did you call it out there?” he asked. Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline. Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste
Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent
Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.” Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.” “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.” Jhace’s jaw worked. He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board. He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.” Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked. She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational. “Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.
18 A knock hit the workshop door. Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded. “Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.” Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?” “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.” Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.” “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.” Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.” Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.” “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said. Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.” Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’” Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.” Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.” Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards. “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
19

sections=[]
for idx,m in enumerate(matches):
    start=m.start()
    head=m.group(1).strip()
    body_start=m.end()
    end = matches[idx+1].start() if idx+1<len(matches) else len(txt)
    body=txt[body_start:end].strip("\n")
    sections.append((head, body))

[ASSISTANT]
def parse_head(head):
    ch=re.match(r'##\s+CHAPTER\s+(\d+):\s*(.*)', head)
    if ch:
        return ("chapter", int(ch.group(1)), ch.group(2).strip())
    if head.startswith("## CODA"):
        return ("coda", None, head.replace("##","").strip())
    return ("other", None, head)
selected={}
ordered=[]
for head,body in sections:
    typ,num,title=parse_head(head)
    if typ=="chapter":
        if num not in selected:
            selected[num]=(title, body)
            ordered.append(num)
    elif typ=="coda":
        selected['coda']=(title, body)
# ensure all 1-13 present
missing=[n for n in range(1,14) if n not in selected]
missing, ordered[:5], ordered[-3:]

from docx.shared import Inches
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

def clean_paras(text):
    text=text.strip()
    # unify newlines
    text=text.replace('\r\n','\n').replace('\r','\n')
    # split paragraphs on blank lines
    parts=re.split(r'\n\s*\n+', text)
    paras=[]
    for p in parts:
        p=p.strip()
        if not p:
            continue
        # join internal newlines
        p=re.sub(r'\s*\n\s*', ' ', p)
        # collapse spaces
        p=re.sub(r'[ \t]+', ' ', p).strip()
        paras.append(p)
    return paras

# apply chapter12 fix
title12, body12 = selected[12]
body12_fixed = body12.replace('“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\n\n', '')
selected[12]=(title12, body12_fixed)

# build doc
out_doc=Document()
# set default font
style=out_doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name='Calibri'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Calibri')
style.font.size=Pt(11)

# Title
p=out_doc.add_paragraph("N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS")
p.style=out_doc.styles['Title']
p.alignment=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

# add chapters
for n in range(1,14):
    title, body = selected[n]
    heading_text=f"CHAPTER {n}: {title}".strip()
    out_doc.add_paragraph(heading_text, style='Heading 1')
    for para in clean_paras(body):
        out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')
    # blank line between chapters
    out_doc.add_paragraph("", style='Normal')

# coda
coda_title, coda_body = selected['coda']
out_doc.add_paragraph("CODA", style='Heading 1')
for para in clean_paras(coda_body):
    out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')

# final end line if present in body? the coda body ends with END line maybe.
# Ensure end line included; check
end_present="END N01" in coda_body
end_present

from docx import Document
path="/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_CLEAN.docx"
doc=Document(path)
# collect all text runs for search
terms=["west","east","north","south","degrees","degree","°","minute","hour","day","week","month","year","second","today","tomorrow","yesterday"]
hits=[]
for pi,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    text=p.text
    low=text.lower()
    for t in terms:
        if t in low:
            hits.append((pi,t,text))
hits[:20], len(hits)

[TOOL]
# DOCX reading, creation, and review guidance

## Reading DOCXs
- Use `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX` to convert DOCXs to PDFs.
  - The `-env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$` flag is important. Otherwise, it will time out.
- Then Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- Then open the PNGs and read the images.
- Only do python printing as a last resort because you will miss important details with text extraction (e.g. figures, tables, diagrams).

## Primary tooling for creating DOCXs
- Create and edit DOCX files with `python-docx`. Use it to control structure, styles, tables, and lists. Install it with `pip install python-docx` if it's not already installed.
- After **every meaningful batch of edits**—new sections, layout tweaks, styling changes—render the DOCX to PDF:
  - `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX`
- Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- **Inspect every PNG before moving on.** If you see any defect, fix the DOCX and repeat the render → inspect loop until all pages look perfect.


## Quality expectations
- Aim for a client-ready document: consistent typography, spacing, margins, and layout hierarchy. Heading levels should be obvious, lists aligned, and paragraphs easy to scan.
- Never ship obvious formatting defects such as clipped or overlapping text, default-template styling, broken tables, unreadable characters, or inconsistent bullet styling.
- Charts, tables, and visuals must be legible in the rendered PNGs—no pixelation, misalignment, missing labels, or mismatched colors.
- Never use the U+2011 non-breaking hyphen or other unicode dashes as they will not be rendered correctly. Use ASCII hyphens instead.
- Citations, references, and footnotes must be human-readable and professional. No tool-internal tokens (e.g., `[145036110387964†L158-L160]`), malformed URLs, or placeholder text should be present in the document.
- You must convert all citations into a human-readable format in the document with standard scholarly citation format. No `【【turn1541736113682297662view0†L11-L19】` notations are allowed in the document as the reader cannot interpret them (such citations will be severely penalized).
- Content should be concise, relevant, and free of boilerplate AI phrasing. Ensure each section adds value and flows logically.

## Final checks
- Re-run the DOCX → PDF → PNG loop after your final changes and inspect **every page** at 100% zoom. Look for subtle issues like inconsistent spacing, widows/orphans, or misaligned bullet levels.
- Correct every formatting defect you see in the PNGs, including but not limited to: overlapping text or shapes, clipped text or shapes that are cut off, black squares, broken tables, unreadable characters, etc.
- Only deliver the DOCX once the latest PNG review confirms the document is visually flawless and professionally styled.
- Keep intermediate files organized (or cleaned up) so reviewers can easily locate final outputs.

Seconds lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug for hours. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. unique=sorted(set(t for _,t,_ in hits))

[TOOL]
['day',
 'degree',
 'degrees',
 'east',
 'hour',
 'minute',
 'second',
