[ASSISTANT]
for pi,t,text in hits:
    if t in ["degrees","degree","west","east","south","day","hour","minute","second","today"]:
        print(pi, t, text)

[TOOL]
d in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.
148 second Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.
150 day --- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.
156 day The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[TOOL]
placement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.

--- paragraph 53 terms {'east'} ---
A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

--- paragraph 58 terms {'hour'} ---
Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---

--- paragraph 150 terms {'day'} ---

Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

--- paragraph 156 terms {'day'} ---
The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[ASSISTANT]
def find_paras_containing(word):
    res=[]
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        if word.lower() in p.text.lower():
            res.append((i,p.text))
    return res

for w in ["west","east","south","north","degrees","degree","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds","today","day","days"]:
    res=find_paras_containing(w)
    if res:
        print(w,len(res),[r[0] for r in res[:10]])

south 1 [35]

hour 6 [4, 8, 15, 27, 58, 114]
hours 5 [4, 15, 27, 58, 114]
minute 3 [4, 14, 15]
minutes 3 [4, 14, 15]
second 10 [8, 13, 15, 25, 27, 31, 35, 48, 49, 148]
seconds 2 [15, 27]
today 1 [8]
day 4 [8, 148, 150, 156]
days 2 [150, 156]

[ASSISTANT]
important_idxs=sorted(set([4,8,13,14,15,25,27,31,35,48,49,53,58,114,148,150,156]))
for pi in important_idxs:
    print("\n###",pi)
    print(doc.paragraphs[pi].text)

[TOOL]
eryone else forgetting how to breathe. Just light—copper‑gold, dense, not filament‑thin. It didn’t thread into the structure like Jhace’s holds did. It sat inside the beam like a replacement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.

### 53
A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

### 58
Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---

### 114
Tiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.

### 148
Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.

### 150
--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

49 Jhace’s tremor pulsed like a warning. He didn’t know the Shard‑Bearer. He could already feel the change—in the way strangers leaned back from doorways, in the way foremen stopped pretending they weren’t watching. --- Jhace saw him in Sector Twelve. Not by choice. They were moving toward another assessment call when the crowd parted the wrong way—like people avoided a spill in the street. A grain store had folded inward. One beam still held the second floor up at an angle that should have been impossible. A man stood under it with both hands on the timber. Alone. No spotters. No wedges. No shouting counts. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.

WORD seconds
15 The tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle. The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting. A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise. "You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again." Jhace pulled free. "I don't—" "There's forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got." The copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again. His chest heat flared. The doubled harmonic he'd felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned like they were the same system. He didn't want this. He didn't understand this. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore. "Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting." He stepped under the sagging upper floor. The heat detonated behind his sternum. He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it. The warehouse stopped settling. The groan of stressed iron went quiet. Jhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response. The tremor in his arms worsened. Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen. "Move!" someone shouted. "Get them out!" Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble. A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth. She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders. "You're the Tallow Street hold," she said. Not a question. A confirmation. "Yeah," Jhace managed. "How long have you been holding?" "I don't know. Five minutes? Ten?" Her pen scratched. "Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible." "What?" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it. "Your body is paying for this," she said, still writing even as her thumb cramped. "If it locks past tolerance, you won’t be able to let go." "I can't let go," Jhace said through clenched teeth. "Not until they're out." The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. "Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside." "Then I hold for forty." "Then you might not walk away." Jhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. "I already know that." The woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror. "I'm documenting this," she said. "Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why." "What's your name?" Jhace asked. "Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory." "Jhace Torrins." "I know," Tiffani said. "I wrote it down at Tallow Street." She showed him her slate: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive. Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density. Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data. "What caused this?" he asked. Tiffani's pen paused. "Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—" She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight. "—everywhere." Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him. "The doubled harmonic," he said. "You felt it too?" Tiffani's eyes sharpened. "In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch." Tiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation. A rescue crew leader shouted. "Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned." Jhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked. "How long have I been holding?" he rasped. Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. "Eighteen minutes." "Tallow Street was four." "Your cost is accelerating," Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. "Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable." Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip. "Terminate after extraction," Tiffani said. "Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go." "What if I can't?" "Then I make you." The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely. "Clear!" the crew leader yelled. Tiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real. "Terminate," she said. "Now." Jhace let the filament go. The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty. Jhace staggered back. His legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone. Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving. Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination). Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded. Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. "You write everything like it's going to behave." "Everything does behave," Tiffani said. "Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria." "It's killing me in increments." "Yes," Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. "So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray." Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data. "Why are you doing this?" he asked. Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain. "Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it," she said. "Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why." A bell rang in the distance. Fourth collapse. Different sector. The copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth. The heat in his chest flared. Tiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating. She looked at Jhace. "Can you stand?" "I don't know." "Try." Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood. "Good," Tiffani said. "Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat." She walked toward the sound of the bell. Jhace followed. Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat. The bell kept ringing.
27 The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug for hours. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
import re, textwrap
def contexts(word, window=60):
    out=[]
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        txt=p.text
        for m in re.finditer(re.escape(word), txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE):
            start=max(0,m.start()-window)
            end=min(len(txt), m.end()+window)
            out.append((i, txt[start:end]))
    return out

for w in ["degrees","west","hour","minute","today","day","days","second","seconds"]:
    ctx=contexts(w)
    if ctx:
        print("\n==",w,"==")
        for i,snip in ctx[:10]:
            print(i, snip.replace("\n"," "))

[ASSISTANT]
import re
def find_word(word):
    res=[]
    pat=re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(word)}\b", re.IGNORECASE)
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        if pat.search(p.text):
            res.append((i,p.text))
    return res
for w in ["day","days","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds","today","tomorrow","yesterday","west","east","north","south","degree","degrees"]:
    res=find_word(w)
    if res:
        print(w, len(res), [i for i,_ in res])

150 --- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[ASSISTANT]
from copy import deepcopy
import re
doc2=Document(path)

def set_paragraph_text(paragraph, text):
    # preserve paragraph style
    p=paragraph
    # remove all runs
    for r in p.runs[::-1]:
        p._element.remove(r._element)
    p.add_run(text)

# paragraph-specific edits
def edit_para(i, func):
    p=doc2.paragraphs[i]
    new=func(p.text)
    set_paragraph_text(p,new)

# 4
edit_para(4, lambda t: re.sub(r"\bfor hours\b", "for too long", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))
# 8
def fix8(t):
    t=re.sub(r"\b(today)\b", "this Tide", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    t=re.sub(r"\bthis morning\b", "earlier this Tide", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    t=re.sub(r"\bthe last hour\b", "this Tide", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    t=re.sub(r"\bfor a second\b", "for a heartbeat", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # degrees west
    t=re.sub(r"(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*degrees\s*west", r"\1 marks off true", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    t=re.sub(r"(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*degrees", r"\1 marks", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return t
edit_para(8, fix8)
# 15
def fix15(t):
    t=re.sub(r"\btwo hours ago\b", "earlier this Tide", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    t=re.sub(r"Duration:\s*19 minutes,\s*40 seconds\.", "Duration: 1,180 heartbeats.", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return t
edit_para(15, fix15)
# 27
edit_para(27, lambda t: re.sub(r"\bfor hours\b", "until their arms went numb", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))
# 58
edit_para(58, lambda t: re.sub(r"\btoo many hours on stone floors\b", "too many stands on stone floors", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))
# 114
edit_para(114, lambda t: re.sub(r"\bhours without sleep\b", "too long without sleep", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))
# 156
edit_para(156, lambda t: re.sub(r"\bfor days\b", "for multiple Tides", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))

# global conversions for remaining minute/second/hour/day cardinal etc
minute_pat=re.compile(r"\b(\d+)\s*minutes\b", re.IGNORECASE)
second_pat=re.compile(r"\b(\d+)\s*seconds\b", re.IGNORECASE)
hour_pat=re.compile(r"\b(\d+)\s*hours\b", re.IGNORECASE)
hour1_pat=re.compile(r"\b(\d+)\s*hour\b", re.IGNORECASE)

def convert_units(text):
    text=re.sub(r"\bfor a second\b", "for a heartbeat", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # convert minutes to heartbeats (60 per minute)
    def rep_min(m):
        n=int(m.group(1))
        return f"{n*60} heartbeats"
    text=minute_pat.sub(rep_min, text)
    def rep_sec(m):
        n=int(m.group(1))
        return f"{n} heartbeats"
    text=second_pat.sub(rep_sec, text)
    # convert numeric hours to Tides vaguely: use "Tides" but avoid precise mapping; better: "{n} Tides"?? hours are sub-Tide; so use "stretches"
    def rep_hr(m):
        n=int(m.group(1))
        return f"{n} stretches"
    text=hour_pat.sub(rep_hr, text)
    text=hour1_pat.sub(lambda m: f"{m.group(1)} stretch", text)
    # standalone 'today' etc
    text=re.sub(r"\btoday\b", "this Tide", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    text=re.sub(r"\bthis morning\b", "earlier this Tide", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # remove cardinal directions if any: west/east/north/south -> directional relative terms
    text=re.sub(r"\bwest\b", "down-terrace", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    text=re.sub(r"\beast\b", "up-terrace", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    text=re.sub(r"\bnorth\b", "upperward", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    text=re.sub(r"\bsouth\b", "lowerward", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # degrees alone
    text=re.sub(r"\bdegrees\b", "marks", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    text=re.sub(r"\bdegree\b", "mark", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return text

for p in doc2.paragraphs:
    new=convert_units(p.text)
    if new!=p.text:
        set_paragraph_text(p,new)

# verify no forbidden words remain (word-boundary)
forbidden=["day","days","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds","today","tomorrow","yesterday","west","east","north","south","degree","degrees"]
remaining={}
for w in forbidden:
    res=[]
    pat=re.compile(rf"\b{w}\b", re.IGNORECASE)
    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):
        if pat.search(p.text):
            res.append(i)
    if res:
        remaining[w]=res
remaining

def snippets_for(word):
    pat=re.compile(rf"\b{word}\b", re.IGNORECASE)
    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):
        m=pat.search(p.text)
        if m:
            txt=p.text
            print(i, txt[max(0,m.start()-60):m.end()+60])
print("days"); snippets_for("days")
print("minutes"); snippets_for("minutes")
print("second"); snippets_for("second")
print("seconds"); snippets_for("seconds")

[ASSISTANT]
word_to_int={"one":1,"two":2,"three":3,"four":4,"five":5,"six":6,"seven":7,"eight":8,"nine":9,"ten":10,
             "eleven":11,"twelve":12,"thirteen":13,"fourteen":14,"fifteen":15,"sixteen":16,"seventeen":17,"eighteen":18,"nineteen":19,"twenty":20}

def convert_word_minutes(text):
    # e.g., "five minutes" -> "300 heartbeats"
    for w,n in word_to_int.items():
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+minutes\b", f"{n*60} heartbeats", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+minute\b", f"{n*60} heartbeats", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+seconds\b", f"{n} heartbeats", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+second\b", f"{n} heartbeats", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+hours\b", f"{n} stretches", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        text=re.sub(rf"\b{w}\s+hour\b", f"{n} stretch", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return text

# apply across all paragraphs
for p in doc2.paragraphs:
    new=convert_word_minutes(p.text)
    if new!=p.text:
        set_paragraph_text(p,new)

# paragraph-specific remaining fixes
# 4: "didn't have minutes" -> "didn't have margin"
p=doc2.paragraphs[4]
if re.search(r"\bminutes\b", p.text, re.IGNORECASE):
    set_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r"\bminutes\b", "margin", p.text, flags=re.IGNORECASE))

# 150: replace "for days" -> "for multiple Tides"
p=doc2.paragraphs[150]
set_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r"\bfor days\b", "for multiple Tides", p.text, flags=re.IGNORECASE))

# fix 'Seconds lied...' in paragraph 27
p=doc2.paragraphs[27]
set_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r"\bSeconds\b", "Counts", p.text))

# replace various 'second' occurrences
def replace_second_everywhere():
    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):
        txt=p.text
        new=txt
        new=re.sub(r"\bsecond heartbeat\b", "shadow heartbeat", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        new=re.sub(r"\bCHAPTER 3:\s*SECOND HOLD\b", "CHAPTER 3: HOLD TWO", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        new=re.sub(r"\bSecond manifestation\b", "Follow-on manifestation", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        new=re.sub(r"\bthe second was\b", "Next was", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        new=re.sub(r"\bsecond floor\b", "upper floor", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        new=re.sub(r"\bsecond stack\b", "next stack", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        # standalone 'second' (as ordinal/time) -> 'next' when plausible
        new=re.sub(r"\bsecond\b", "next", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        if new!=txt:
            set_paragraph_text(p,new)
replace_second_everywhere()

# recheck forbidden list (word boundary)

        remaining[w]=res

"The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway."
